factor A into $L U :$

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 2 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } & { 2 } \\ { 6 } & { 3 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] .$$

6
What two elimination matrices $E _ { 2 1 }$ and $E _ { 3 2 }$ put $A$ into upper triangular form
$E _ { 3 2 } E _ { 2 1 } A = U$ Multiply by $E _ { 3 2 } ^ { - 1 }$ and $E _ { 2 1 } ^ { - 1 }$ to factor $A$ into $L U = E _ { 2 1 } ^ { - 1 } E _ { 3 2 } ^ { - 1 } U :$

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 4 } & { 5 } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] .$$

7
What three elimination matrices $E _ { 2 1 } ,$ $E _ { 3 1 } ,$ $E _ { 3 2 }$ put A into its upper triangular form

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 2 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] \quad L = E _ { 2 1 } ^ { - 1 } E _ { 3 1 } ^ { - 1 } E _ { 3 2 } ^ { - 1 } .$$

Suppose $A$ is already lower triangular with 1's on the diagonal. Then $U = I !$

$$A = L = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { a } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { b } & { c } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] .$$

The elimination matrices $E _ { 2 1 } ,$ $E _ { 3 1 } ,$ $E _ { 3 2 }$ contain $- a$ then $- b$ then $- c .$

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { d } & { e } \\ { 0 } & { f } \end{array} \right] \quad \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { m } & { n } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { d } & { e } & { 8 } \\ { f } & { f } & { h } \end{array} \right]$$

This difficulty is fixed by a row exchange. That needs a "permutation" $P .$

#### 2.7 Transposes and Permutations

We need one more matrix, and fortunately it is much simpler than the inverse. It is the
"transpose" of $A ,$ which is denoted by $A ^ { T } .$ The columns of $A ^ { T }$ are the rows of A.

When A is an $m$ by $n$ matrix, the transpose is $n$ by $m :$

Transpose

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] \text { then } A ^ { T }$$
= 2 0 .

1
3
0
4

You can write the rows of $A$ into the columns of $A ^ { T } .$ Or you can write the columns of A
into the rows of $A ^ { T } .$ The matrix "flips over" its main diagonal. The entry in row $i ,$ column $j$
of $A ^ { T }$ comes from row $j ,$ column $i$ of the original A:

Exchange rows and columns

$$\left( A ^ { T } \right) _ { i j } = A _ { j i } .$$

The transpose of a lower triangular matrix is upper triangular. (But the inverse is still lower
triangular.) The transpose of $A ^ { T }$ is A.

Note MATLAB's symbol for the transpose of $A$ is $A ^ { \prime } .$ Typing [ 1 2 3] gives a row vec-
tor and the column vector is $v = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] ^ { \prime } .$ To enter a matrix $M$ with second column
$w = \left[ 4 5 6 \right] ^ { \prime }$ you could define $M = \left[ \begin{array}{} { v } & { w } \end{array} \right]$ ]. Quicker to enter by rows and then
transpose the whole matrix: $M = \left[ 1 2 3 ; 4 5 6 \right] ^ { \prime } .$

The rules for transposes are very direct. We can transpose $A + B$ to get $\left( A + B \right) ^ { T } .$
$O r$ we can transpose A and $B$ separately, and then add $A ^ { T } + B ^ { T }$ BT-with the same result.
The serious questions are about the transpose of a product $A B$ and an inverse $A ^ { - 1 } :$

The transpose of is $A ^ { T } + B ^ { T } .$
$$A + B$$
(1)

<tr>
<td>Product</td>
<td>The transpose of AB is $\left( A B \right) ^ { T } = B ^ { T } A ^ { T }$</td>
<td>(2)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Inverse</td>
<td>The transpose of $A ^ { - 1 }$ is $\left( A ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { T } = \left( A ^ { T } \right) ^ { - 1 } .$</td>
<td>(3)</td>
</tr>
</table>


Notice especially how $B ^ { T } A ^ { T }$ comes in reverse order. For inverses, this reverse order
was quick to check: $B ^ { - 1 } A ^ { - 1 }$ times $A B$ produces $I .$ To understand $\left( A B \right) ^ { T } = B ^ { T } A ^ { T } ,$
start with $\left( A x \right) ^ { T } = x ^ { T } A ^ { T } :$

$A x$ combines the columns of A while $x ^ { T } A ^ { T }$ combines the rows of $A ^ { T } .$

It is the same combination of the same vectors! $\mathrm { I n } A$ they are columns, in $A ^ { T }$ they are rows.
So the transpose of the column $A x$ is the row $x ^ { T } A ^ { T } .$ That fits our formula $\left( A x \right) ^ { T } = x ^ { T } A ^ { T } .$
Now we can prove the formula $\left( A B \right) ^ { T } = B ^ { T } A ^ { T } ,$ when $B$ has several columns.

If $B = \left[ x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } \right]$ has two columns, apply the same idea to each column. The columns
of $A B$ are $A x _ { 1 }$ and $A x _ { 2 } .$ Their transposes are the rows of $B ^ { T } A ^ { T } :$

$$\mathrm { T r a n s p o s i n g } A B = \left[ \begin{array}{} { } _ { A x _ { 1 } } A x _ { 2 } & \ldots \\ A x _ { 1 } & A x _ { 2 } & \ldots \end{array} \right] \mathrm { g i v e s } \left[ \begin{array}{} { x _ { 1 } ^ { T } A ^ { T } } \\ { x _ { 2 } ^ { T } A ^ { T } } \\ { x _ { 2 } ^ { T } A ^ { T } } \end{array} \right] \text { which is } B ^ { T } A ^ { T }$$
. (4)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="108" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 2. Solving Linear Equations" -->

The right answer $B ^ { T } A ^ { T }$ comes out a row at a time. Here are numbers in $\left( A B \right) ^ { T } = B ^ { T } A ^ { T } :$

$A B = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 5 } & { 0 } \\ { 4 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 5 } & { 0 } \\ { 9 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$ and $B ^ { T } A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 5 } & { 4 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 5 } & { 9 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$

The reverse order rule extends to three or more factors: $\left( A B C \right) ^ { T }$ equals $C ^ { T } B ^ { T } A ^ { T } .$

If $A = L D U$ then $A ^ { T } = U ^ { T } D ^ { T } L ^ { T } .$ The pivot matrix has $D = D ^ { T }$ .

Now apply this product rule to both sides of $A ^ { - 1 } A = I .$ On one side, $I ^ { T }$ is $I .$ We
confirm the rule that $\left( A ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { T }$ is the inverse of $A ^ { T } ,$ because their product is $I$ :

Transpose of inverse $A ^ { - 1 } A = I$ is transposed to AT(A-1)T = I. (5)

Similarly $A A ^ { - 1 } = I$ leads to $\left( A ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { T } A ^ { T } = I .$ We can invert the transpose or we can
transpose the inverse. Notice especially: $A ^ { T }$ is invertible exactly when A is invertible.

Example 1 The inverse of $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 6 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$ is $A ^ { - 1 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { - 6 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] .$ The transpose is $A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 6 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] .$

$$\left( A ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { T } \text { and } \left( A ^ { T } \right) ^ { - 1 } \text { are both equal to } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { - 6 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] .$$


### The Meaning of Inner Products

We know the dot product (inner product) of $x$ and $y .$ It is the sum of numbers $x _ { i } y _ { i } .$
Now we have a better way to write $x \cdot y ,$ without using that unprofessional dot. Use
matrix notation instead:

T is inside The dot product or inner product is $x ^ { T } y$
$\left( 1 \times n \right) \left( n \times 1 \right)$
$\mathrm { T }$ is outside The rank one product or outer product is $x y ^ { T }$ $\left( n \times 1 \right) \left( 1 \times n \right)$

$x ^ { T } y$ is a number, $x y ^ { T }$ is a matrix. Quantum mechanics would write those $a s < x | y >$
(inner) and $| x > < y |$ (outer). I think the world is governed by linear algebra, but physics
disguises it well. Here are examples where the inner product has meaning:

$$= \left( \text { Movements } \right) \left( \text { Forces } \right) = x ^ { T } f$$
From mechanics


<table>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>From circuits</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>From economics</td>
<td>$$\mathrm { H e a t } \mathrm { l o s s } = \left( \text { Voltage drops } \right) \left( \text { Currents } \right) = e ^ { T } y$$ $$= \left( \text { Quantities } \right) \left( \text { Prices } \right) = q ^ { T } p$$</td>
</tr>
</table>


We are really close to the heart of applied mathematics, and there is one more point to
explain. It is the deeper connection between inner products and the transpose of $A .$

We defined $A ^ { T }$ by flipping the matrix across its main diagonal. That's not mathematics.
There is a better way to approach the transpose. $A ^ { T }$ is the matrix that makes these two
inner products equal for every $x$ and $y$ :

$$\left( A x \right) ^ { T } y = x ^ { T } \left( A ^ { T } y \right)$$
Inner product of $A x$ with y = Inner product of $x$ with $A ^ { T } y$

<!-- PageNumber="109" -->
<!-- PageHeader="2.7. Transposes and Permutations" -->

On one side we have $A x$ multiplying $y : \left( x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right) y _ { 1 } + \left( x _ { 3 } - x _ { 2 } \right) y _ { 2 }$
That is the same as $x _ { 1 } \left( - y _ { 1 } \right) + x _ { 2 } \left( y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } \right) + x _ { 3 } \left( y _ { 2 } \right) .$ Now $x$ is multiplying $A ^ { T } y .$

$A ^ { T } y$ must be y1 - 12 which produces $A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$ as expected.

Example 3 Will you allow me a little calculus? It is extremely important or I wouldn't
leave linear algebra. (This is really linear algebra for functions $\left. x \left( t \right) . \right)$ The difference ma-
trix changes to a derivative $A = d / d t .$ Its transpose will now come from $\left( d x / d t , y \right) =$
$\left( x , - d y / d t \right) .$

The inner product changes from a finite sum of $x _ { k } y _ { k }$ to an integral of $x \left( t \right) y \left( t \right) .$

Inner product
of functions

$$x ^ { T } y = \left( x , y \right) = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } x \left( t \right) y d t$$

$\begin{array}{} { \text { Transpose rule } } \\ { \left( A x \right) ^ { T } y = x ^ { T } \left( A ^ { T } y \right) \quad \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { d x } { d t } y \left( t \right) d t = \int _ { - \infty } ^ { \infty } x \left( t \right) \left( - \frac { d y } { d t } \right) d t } \end{array}$

(6)

I hope you recognize "integration by parts". The derivative moves from the first
function $x \left( t \right)$ to the second function $y \left( t \right) .$ During that move, a minus sign appears.
This tells us that the "transpose" of the derivative is minus the derivative.

The derivative is anti-symmetric: $A = d / d t$ and $A ^ { T } = - d / d t .$ Symmetric matrices
have $A ^ { T } = A ,$ anti-symmetric matrices have $A ^ { T } = - A .$ In some way, the 2 by 3 difference
matrix above followed this pattern. The 3 by 2 matrix $A ^ { T }$ was minus a difference matrix.
It produced $y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 }$ in the middle component of $A ^ { T } y$ instead of the difference $y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } .$


## Symmetric Matrices

For a symmetric matrix, transposing A to $A ^ { T }$ produces no change. Then $A ^ { T } = A .$ Its $\left( j , i \right)$
entry across the main diagonal equals its $\left( i , j \right)$ entry. In my opinion, these are the most
important matrices of all.

DEFINITION A symmetric matrix has $A ^ { T } = A$ , This means that $a _ { j i } = a _ { i j }$

Symmetric matrices $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 2 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] = A ^ { T }$ and $D = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 0 } \end{array} \right] = D ^ { T } .$

The inverse of a symmetric matrix is also symmetric. The transpose of $A ^ { - 1 }$ is
$\left( A ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { T } = \left( A ^ { T } \right) ^ { - 1 } = A ^ { - 1 } .$ That says $A ^ { - 1 }$ is symmetric (when A is invertible):

$$D ^ { - 1 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 . 1 } \end{array} \right]$$
Symmetric inverses $A ^ { - 1 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 5 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$ and

Now we produce symmetric matrices by multiplying any matrix $R$ by $R ^ { T } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="110" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 2. Solving Linear Equations" -->


## Symmetric Products $R ^ { T } R$ and $R R ^ { T }$ and $L D L ^ { T }$

Choose any matrix $R ,$ probably rectangular. Multiply $R ^ { \mathrm { T } }$ times $R .$ Then the product $R ^ { \mathrm { T } } R$
is automatically a square symmetric matrix:

The transpose of $R ^ { T } R$ is $R ^ { T } \left( R ^ { T } \right) ^ { T }$ which is $R ^ { T } R .$
(7)

That is a quick proof of symmetry for $R ^ { T } R .$ We could also look at the $\left( i , j \right)$ entry of $R ^ { T } R .$
It is the dot product of row $i$ of $R ^ { T }$ (column $i$ of $\left. R \right)$ with column $j$ of $R .$ The $\left( j , i \right)$ entry
is the same dot product, column $j$ with column $i$ i. So $R ^ { T } R$ is symmetric.

The matrix $R R ^ { T }$ is also symmetric. (The shapes of $R$ and $R ^ { T }$ allow multiplication.)
But $R R ^ { T }$ is a different matrix from $R ^ { T } R .$ In our experience, most scientific problems that
start with a rectangular matrix $R$ end up with $R ^ { T } R$ or $R R ^ { T }$ or both. As in least squares.

Example 4 Multiply $R = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 1 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$ and RT = 1 -1 in both orders.
0
-1 0
1

$$R R ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right]$$
and $R ^ { T } R = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { - 1 } & { 0 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } & { - 1 } \\ { 0 } & { - 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$ |are both symmetric matrices.

The product $R ^ { T } R$ is $n$ by n. In the opposite order, $R R ^ { T }$ is $m$ by $m .$ Both are symmetric,
with positive diagonal (why?). But even if $m = n ,$ it is not very likely that $R ^ { T } R = R R ^ { T } .$
Equality can happen, but it is abnormal.

Symmetric matrices in elimination $A ^ { T } = A$ makes elimination faster, because we can
work with half the matrix (plus the diagonal). It is true that the upper triangular $U$ is
probably not symmetric. The symmetry is in the triple product $A = L D U .$ Remember
how the diagonal matrix $D$ of pivots can be divided out, to leave 1's on the diagonal of both
L and $U :$

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 2 } & { 7 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]$$

L U misses the symmetry of A

$$= \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$$

When A is symmetric, the usual form $A = L D U$ becomes $A = L D L ^ { T } .$ The final $U$
(with 1's on the diagonal) is the transpose of $L$ (also with 1's on the diagonal). The
diagonal matrix $D$ containing the pivots is symmetric by itself.

If $A = A T$ is factored into LDU with no row exchanges, then U is exactly $L ^ { T } .$

The symmetric factorization of a symmetric matrix is $A = L D L ^ { T } .$

Notice that the transpose of $L D L ^ { T }$ is automatically $\left( L ^ { T } \right) ^ { T } D ^ { T } L ^ { T }$ which is $L D L ^ { T }$ again.
The work of elimination is cut in half, from $n ^ { 3 } / 3$ multiplications to $n ^ { 3 } / 6 .$ The storage is
also cut essentially in half. We only keep $L$ and $D ,$ not $U$ which is just $L ^ { T } .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="111" -->
<!-- PageHeader="2.7. Transposes and Permutations" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Permutation Matrices" -->

The transpose plays a special role for a permutation matrix. This matrix $P$ has a single "1"
in every row and every column. Then $P ^ { \mathrm { T } }$ is also a permutation matrix-maybe the same
or maybe different. Any product $P _ { 1 } P _ { 2 }$ is again a permutation matrix. We now create every
$P$ from the identity matrix, by reordering the rows of $I .$

The simplest permutation matrix is $P = I$ (no exchanges). The next simplest are the
row exchanges $P _ { i j } .$ Those are constructed by exchanging two rows $i$ and $j$ of $I .$ Other
permutations reorder more rows. By doing all possible row exchanges to $I ,$ we get all
possible permutation matrices:


### DEFINITION A permutation matrix $\bar { P }$ has the rows of the identity $I$ in any order,

Example 5 There are six 3 by 3 permutation matrices. Here they are without the zeros:

I =
P31 =

·

There are $n !$ permutation matrices of order n. The symbol $n !$ means "n factorial," the
product of the numbers $\left( 1 \right) \left( 2 \right) \cdots \left( n \right) .$ Thus $3 ! = \left( 1 \right) \left( 2 \right) \left( 3 \right)$ which is 6. There will be 24
permutation matrices of order $n = 4 .$ And 120 permutations of order 5.

There are only two permutation matrices of order 2, namely $\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$ and $\left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] .$

Important: $P ^ { - 1 }$ is also a permutation matrix. Among the six 3 by 3 $P s$ displayed
above, the four matrices on the left are their own inverses. The two matrices on the right
are inverses of each other. In all cases, a single row exchange is its own inverse. If we
repeat the exchange we are back to $I .$ But for $P _ { 3 2 } P _ { 2 1 } ,$ the inverses go in opposite order
as always. The inverse is $P _ { 2 1 } P _ { 3 2 } .$

More important: $P ^ { - 1 }$ is always the same as $P ^ { T } .$ The two matrices on the right are
transposes-and inverses-of each other. When we multiply $P P ^ { T } ,$ the "1" in the first row
of $P$ hits the "1" in the first column of $P ^ { T }$ (since the first row of $P$ is the first column of
$P ^ { T } .$ It misses the ones in all the other columns. So $P P ^ { T } = I .$

Another proof of $P ^ { T } = P ^ { - 1 }$ looks at $P$ as a product of row exchanges. Every row
exchange is its own transpose and its own inverse. $P ^ { T }$ and $P ^ { - 1 }$ both come from the
product of row exchanges in reverse order. So $P ^ { T }$ and $P ^ { - 1 }$ are the same.

Symmetric matrices led to $A = L D L ^ { T } .$ Now permutations lead to $P A = L U .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="112" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 2. Solving Linear Equations" -->


## The $P A = L U$ Factorization with Row Exchanges

We sure hope you remember $A = L \quad U .$ It started with $A = \left( E _ { 2 1 } ^ { - 1 } \cdots E _ { i j } ^ { - 1 } \cdots \right) U .$ Every
elimination step was carried out by an $E _ { i j }$ and it was inverted by $E _ { i j } ^ { - 1 } .$ Those inverses were
compressed into one matrix $L ,$ bringing $U$ back to $A .$ The lower triangular $L$ has 1's on
the diagonal, and the result is $A = L \quad U .$

This is a great factorization, but it doesn't always work. Sometimes row exchanges
are needed to produce pivots. Then $A = \left( E ^ { - 1 } \cdots P ^ { - 1 } \cdots E ^ { - 1 } \cdots P ^ { - 1 } \cdots \right) U .$ Every row
exchange is carried out by a $P _ { i j }$ and inverted by that $P _ { i j } .$ We now compress those row ex-
changes into a single permutation matrix $P .$ This gives a factorization for every invertible
matrix A-which we naturally want.

The main question is where to collect the $P _ { i j } .$ There are two good possibilities-
do all the exchanges before elimination, or do them after the $E _ { i j } .$ The first way gives
$P A = L U .$ The second way has a permutation matrix $P _ { 1 }$ in the middle.

1\. The row exchanges can be done $i n$ advance. Their product $P$ puts the rows of A in
the right order, so that no exchanges are needed for $P A .$ Then $P A = L \quad U .$

2\. If we hold row exchanges until after elimination, the pivot rows are in a strange order.
$P _ { 1 }$ puts them in the correct triangular order in $U _ { 1 } .$ Then $A = L _ { 1 } P _ { 1 } U _ { 1 } .$

$P A = L U$ is constantly used in all computing (and in MATLAB). We will concentrate on
this form. Most numerical analysts have never seen the other form.

The factorization $A = L _ { 1 } P _ { 1 } U _ { 1 }$ might be more elegant. If we mention both, it is because
the difference is not well known. Probably you will not spend a long time on either one.
Please don't. The most important case has $P = I ,$ when A equals $L \quad U$ with no exchanges.

For this matrix A, exchange rows 1 and 2 to put the first pivot in its usual place.
Then go through elimination on $P A :$

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 7 } & { 9 } \end{array} \right] \rightarrow \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 7 } & { 9 } \end{array} \right] \rightarrow \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 3 } & { 7 } \\ { 0 } & { 3 } & { 7 } \end{array} \right] \rightarrow \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 4 } \\ { 3 2 } & { = 3 } \end{array} \right]$$

The matrix $P A$ has its rows in good order, and it factors as usual into $L \quad U :$

$$P = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \quad P A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = L U$$
(8)

We started with A and ended with $U .$ The only requirement is invertibility of $A .$

If A is invertible, a permutation $P$ will put its rows in the right order to factor $P A = L \quad U$
There must be a full set of pivots after row exchanges for A to be invertible.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="113" -->
<!-- PageHeader="2.7. Transposes and Permutations" -->

In MATLAB, $A \left( \left[ r \quad k \right] , : \right) = A \left( \left[ k \quad r \right] , : \right)$ exchanges row $k$ with row $r$ below it (where the
kth pivot has been found). Then the lu code updates L and $P$ and the sign of $P :$

$$A \left( \left[ r \quad k \right] , : \right) = A \left( \left[ k \quad r \right] , : \right) ;$$

$$L \left( \left[ r \quad k \right] , 1 : k - 1 \right) = L \left( \left[ k r \right] , 1 : k - 1 \right) ;$$


<table>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>This is part of</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td rowspan="2">$\left[ L , U , P \right] = \mathrm { I u } \left( A \right)$</td>
<td>P([r k], :) = P([k r], :);</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$\mathrm { s i g n } = - \mathrm { s i g n }$</td>
</tr>
</table>


The "sign" of $P$ tells whether the number of row exchanges is even $\left( \mathrm { s i g n } = + 1 \right) .$
An odd number of row exchanges will produce $\mathrm { s i g n } = - 1 .$ At the start, $P$ is $I$ and sign
$= + 1 .$ When there is a row exchange, the sign is reversed. The final value of sign is the
determinant of $P$ and it does not depend on the order of the row exchanges.

For $P A$ we get back to the familiar $L U .$ This is the usual factorization. In reality,
$\mathrm { I u } \left( A \right)$ often does not use the first available pivot. Mathematically we accept a small pivot-
anything but zero. It is better if the computer looks down the column for the largest pivot.
(Section 9.1 explains why this "partial pivoting" reduces the roundoff error.) Then $P$ may
contain row exchanges that are not algebraically necessary. Still $P A = L U .$

Our advice is to understand permutations but let the computer do the work. Calculations
of $A = L U$ are enough to do by hand, without $P .$ The Teaching Code $\sin \left( A \right)$ factors
$P A = L U$ and $\mathrm { s p l v } \left( A , b \right)$ solves $A x = b$ for any invertible $A .$ The program splu stops if
no pivot can be found in column $k .$ Then $A$ is not invertible.


### REVIEW OF THE KEY IDEAS

1\. The transpose puts the rows of $A$ into the columns of $A ^ { T } .$ Then $\left( A ^ { T } \right) _ { i j } = A _ { j i } .$

2\. The transpose of $A B$ is $B ^ { T } A ^ { T } .$ The transpose of $A ^ { - 1 }$ is the inverse of $A ^ { T } .$

3\. The dot product is $x \cdot y = x ^ { T } y .$ Then $\left( A x \right) ^ { T } y$ equals the dot product $x ^ { T } \left( A ^ { T } y \right) .$

4\. When A is symmetric $\left( A ^ { T } = A \right) ,$ its $L D U$ factorization is symmetric: $A = L D L ^ { T } .$

5\. A permutation matrix $P$ has a 1 in each row and column, and $P ^ { T } = P ^ { - 1 }$

6\. There are $n !$ permutation matrices of size $n$ n. Half even, half $o d d .$

7\. If A is invertible then a permutation $P$ will reorder its rows for $P A = L U .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="114" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 2. Solving Linear Equation:" -->


### · WORKED EXAMPLES

2.7 A Applying the permutation $P$ to the rows of A destroys its symmetry:

$$P = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] \quad A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 4 } & { 5 } \\ { 4 } & { 2 } & { 6 } \\ { 5 } & { 6 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \quad P A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 4 } & { 2 } & { 2 } & { 6 } \\ { 5 } & { 6 } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right]$$

What permutation $Q$ applied to the columns of $P A$ will recover symmetry in $P A Q ?$
The numbers 1,2, 3 must come back to the main diagonal (not necessarily in order).
Show that $Q$ is $P ^ { T } ,$ so that symmetry is saved by $P A Q = P A P ^ { T } .$

Solution To recover symmetry and put $2$ back on the diagonal, column 2 of $P A$
must move to column 1. Column 3 of $P A$ (containing "3") must move to column 2.
Then the "1" moves to the 3, 3 position. The matrix that permutes columns is $Q :$

$$P A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 4 } & { 2 } & { 6 } \\ { 5 } & { 6 } & { 3 } \\ { 1 } & { 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] \quad Q = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] \quad P A Q = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 2 } & { 6 } & { 4 } \\ { 6 } & { 3 } & { 5 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] .$$ ☒

The matrix $Q$ is $P ^ { T } .$ This choice always recovers symmetry, because $P A P ^ { T }$ is guaranteed
to be symmetric. (Its transpose is again $P A P ^ { T }$ The matrix $Q$ is also $P ^ { - 1 } ,$ because the
inverse of every permutation matrix is its transpose.

If $D$ is a diagonal matrix, we are finding that $P D P ^ { T }$ is also diagonal. When $P$ moves
row 1 down to row 3, $P ^ { T }$ on the right will move column 1 to column 3. The $\left( 1 , 1 \right)$ entry
moves down to $\left( 3 , 1 \right)$ and over to $\left( 3 , 3 \right) .$

2.7 B Find the symmetric factorization $A = L D L ^ { T }$ for the matrix A above. Is this $A$
invertible? Find also the $P Q = L \quad U$ factorization for $Q ,$ which needs row exchanges.

Solution To factor A into $L D L ^ { T }$ we eliminate below the pivots:

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 4 } & { 5 } \\ { 4 } & { 2 } & { 6 } \\ { 5 } & { 6 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \rightarrow \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 4 } & { 5 } \\ { 0 } & { - 1 4 } & { - 1 4 } \\ { 0 } & { - 1 4 } & { - 2 2 } \end{array} \right] \rightarrow \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 4 } & { 5 } \\ { 0 } & { - 1 4 } & { - 1 4 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { - 8 } \end{array} \right] = U .$$

The multipliers were $\ell _ { 2 1 } = 4$ and $\ell _ { 3 1 } = 5$ and $\ell _ { 3 2 } = 1 .$ The pivots $1 , - 1 4 , - 8$ go into $D .$
When we divide the rows of $U$ by those pivots, $L ^ { T }$ should appear:

$$\left. \begin{array}{} \text { Symmetric } & { } & { } & { } & { } & { 0 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { \text { factorization } } & { A = L D L ^ { T } } & { = } & { 4 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { \text { when } A } & { = A ^ { T } } & { } & { } & { } & { 5 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } & { 5 } \\ { 4 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 5 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } & { 4 } & { 5 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$$

This matrix $A$ is invertible because it has three pivots. Its inverse is $\left( L ^ { T } \right) ^ { - 1 } D ^ { - 1 } L ^ { - 1 }$ and
$A ^ { - 1 }$ is also symmetric. The numbers 14 and 8 will turn up in the denominators of $A ^ { - 1 } .$
The "determinant" of A is the product of the pivots $\left( 1 \right) \left( - 1 4 \right) \left( - 8 \right) = 1 1 2 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="115" -->
<!-- PageHeader="2.7. Transposes and Permutations" -->

Any permutation matrix $Q$ is invertible. Here elimination needs two row exchanges:

$$Q = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] \quad \rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] \quad \rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] = I$$

With $A = Q ,$ the $P Q = \left( L \right) \left( U \right)$ factorization is the same as $Q ^ { - 1 } Q = \left( I \right) \left( I \right) .$

2.7 C For a rectangular A, this saddle-point matrix $S$ is symmetric and important:

Block matrix
from least squares

$$S = \left[ \begin{array}{} { I } & { A } \\ { A ^ { T } } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] = S ^ { T } \text { has size } m + n$$

Apply block elimination to find a block factorization $S = L D L ^ { T } .$ Then test invertibility:

$$S \text { is invertible } \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad A ^ { \mathrm { T } } A \text { is invertible } \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad A x \neq 0 \text { whenerer } x \neq 0$$

Solution The first block pivot is $I .$ The matrix to multiply row 1 is certainly $A ^ { T } :$

Block elimination
$$S = \left[ \begin{array}{} { I } & { A } \\ { A ^ { T } } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]$$

goes to
I
. This is $U .$

LO -ATA
A

The block pivot matrix $D$ contains $I$ and $- A ^ { T } A .$ Then $L$ and $L ^ { T }$ contain $A ^ { T }$ and A:

Block factorization $S = L D L ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { I } & { 0 } \\ { A ^ { T } } & { I } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { I } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { - A ^ { T } A } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { I } & { A } \\ { 0 } & { I } \end{array} \right]$

$L$ is certainly invertible, with diagonal 1's from $I .$ The inverse of the middle matrix
involves $\left( A ^ { T } A \right) ^ { - 1 } .$ Section 4.2 answers a key question about the matrix $A ^ { T } A :$

When is $A ^ { T } A$ invertible? Answer: A must have independent columns.

Then $A x = 0$ only if $x = 0 .$ Otherwise $A x = 0$ will lead to $A ^ { T } A x = 0 .$

## Chapter 3 Vector Spaces and Subspaces


### 3.1 Spaces of Vectors

To a newcomer, matrix calculations involve a lot of numbers. To you, they involve vectors.
The columns of $A x$ and $A B$ are linear combinations of $n$ vectors-the columns of $A .$
This chapter moves from numbers and vectors to a third level of understanding (the highest
level). Instead of individual columns, we look at "spaces" of vectors. Without seeing vector
spaces and especially their subspaces, you haven't understood everything about $A x = b .$

Since this chapter goes a little deeper, it may seem a little harder. That is natural. We
are looking inside the calculations, to find the mathematics. The author's job is to make it
clear. The chapter ends with the "Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra".

We begin with the most important vector spaces. They are denoted by $R ^ { 1 } ,$ $R ^ { 2 } ,$ $R ^ { 3 } ,$
$R ^ { 4 } , \cdots .$ Each space $R ^ { n }$ consists of a whole collection of vectors. $R ^ { 5 }$ contains all column
vectors with five components. This is called "5-dimensional space".

DEFINITION The space R" consists of all column vectors u with n components.

The components of v;are real numbers, which is the reason for the letter R. A vector whose
$n$ components are complex numbers lies in the space $C ^ { n } .$

The vector space $R ^ { 2 }$ is represented by the usual $x y$ plane. Each vector $v$ in $R ^ { 2 }$ has two
components. The word "space" asks us to think of all those vectors-the whole plane.
Each vector gives the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of a point in the plane: $v = \left( x , y \right) .$

Similarly the vectors in $R ^ { 3 }$ correspond to points $\left( x , y , z \right)$ in three-dimensional space.
The one-dimensional space $R ^ { 1 }$ is a line (like the $x$ axis). As before, we print vectors as a
column between brackets, or along a line using commas and parentheses:

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 4 } \\ { \pi } \end{array} \right] \text { is in } R ^ { 2 } , \quad \left( 1 , 1 , 0 , 1 , 1 \right) \text { is in } R ^ { 5 } , \quad \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 + i } \\ { 1 - i } \end{array} \right] \text { is in } C ^ { 2 } ,$$

The great thing about linear algebra is that it deals easily with five-dimensional space.
We don't draw the vectors, we just need the five numbers (or $n$ numbers).

<!-- PageNumber="120" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="121" -->
<!-- PageHeader="3.1. Spaces of Vectors" -->

To multiply $v$ by 7, multiply every component by 7. Here 7 is a "scalar". To add vectors
in $R ^ { 5 } ,$ add them a component at a time. The two essential vector operations go on inside
the vector space, and they produce linear combinations:

We can add any vectors in $R ^ { n } ,$ and we can multiply any vector $v$ by any scalar $c$ c.

"Inside the vector space" means that the result stays in the space. If $v$ is the vector in $R ^ { 4 }$
with components 1, 0, 0, 1, then $2 v$ is the vector in $R ^ { 4 }$ with components $2 , 0 , 0 , 2 .$ (In this
case 2 is the scalar.) $A$ whole series of properties can be verified in $R ^ { n } .$ The commutative
law is $v + w = w + v ;$ the distributive law is $c \left( v + w \right) = c v + c w .$ There is a unique
"zero vector" satisfying $0 + v = v .$ Those are three of the eight conditions listed at the
start of the problem set.

These eight conditions are required of every vector space. There are vectors other than
column vectors, and vector spaces other than $R ^ { n } ,$ and all vector spaces have to obey the
eight reasonable rules.

A real vector space is a set of "vectors" together with rules for vector addition and for
multiplication by real numbers. The addition and the multiplication must produce vectors
that are in the space. And the eight conditions must be satisfied (which is usually no
problem). Here are three vector spaces other than $R ^ { n } :$

M
The vector space of all real 2 by 2 matrices.

F
The vector space of all real functions f (x).

Z
The vector space that consists only of a zero vector.

$\mathrm { I n } M$ the "vectors" are really matrices. In F the vectors are functions. In Z the only addition
is $0 + 0 = 0 .$ In each case we can add: matrices to matrices, functions to functions, zero
vector to zero vector. We can multiply a matrix by 4 or a function by 4 or the zero vector
by 4. The result is still in $M$ or F or $Z .$ The eight conditions are all easily checked.

The function space F is infinite-dimensional. A smaller function space is $\mathrm { P } ,$ or $\mathbb{P} _ { n } ,$
containing all polynomials $a _ { 0 } + a _ { 1 } x + \cdots + a _ { n } x ^ { n }$ of degree $n$ n.

The space $Z$ is zero-dimensional (by any reasonable definition of dimension). It is the
smallest possible vector space. We hesitate to call it $R ^ { 0 } ,$ which means no components-
you might think there was no vector. The vector space $Z$ contains exactly one vector (zero).
No space can do without that zero vector. Each space has its own zero vector-the zero
matrix, the zero function, the vector $\left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right)$ in $R ^ { 3 } .$


## Subspaces

At different times, we will ask you to think of matrices and functions as vectors. But at all
times, the vectors that we need most are ordinary column vectors. They are vectors with
$n$ components-but maybe not all of the vectors with $n$ components. There are important
vector spaces inside $R ^ { n } .$ Those are subspaces of $R ^ { n } .$

Start with the usual three-dimensional space $R ^ { 3 } .$ Choose a plane through the origin
$\left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right) .$ That plane is a vector space in its own right. If we add two vectors in the plane,
their sum is in the plane. If we multiply an in-plane vector by 2 or -5, it is still in the plane.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="122" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 3. Vector Spaces and Subspaces" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 3.1: "Four-dimensional" matrix space $M .$ The "zero-dimensional" space $Z .$</figcaption>

$\left[ \begin{array}{} { a } & { b } \\ { c } & { d } \end{array} \right] = \text { typical vector in } M$

$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$

$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]$

$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]$

smallest vector space
zero vector only

$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]$

</figure>


A plane in three-dimensional space is not $R ^ { 2 }$ (even if it looks like $R ^ { 2 } .$ The vectors have
three components and they belong to $R ^ { 3 } .$ The plane is a vector space inside $R ^ { 3 } .$

This illustrates one of the most fundamental ideas in linear algebra. The plane going
through $\left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right)$ is a subspace of the full vector space $R ^ { 3 } .$

DEFINITION A subspace of a vector space is a set of vectors (including 0) that satisfies
two requirements: If v and w are vectors in the subspace and $c$ is any scalar, then

(i) $v + w$ is in the subspace

(ii) $c v$ is in the subspace.

In other words, the set of vectors is "closed" under addition $v + w$ and multiplication $c v$
(and $\left. c w \right) .$ Those operations leave us in the subspace. We can also subtract, because $- w$ is
in the subspace and its sum with $v$ is $v \quad - \quad w .$ In short, all linear combinations stay in the
subspace.

All these operations follow the rules of the host space, so the eight required conditions
are automatic. We just have to check the requirements for a subspace, so that we can take
linear combinations.

First fact: Every subspace contains the zero vector. The plane in $R ^ { 3 }$ has to go through
$\left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right) .$ We mention this separately, for extra emphasis, but it follows directly from rule (ii).
Choose $c = 0 ,$ and the rule requires Ov to be in the subspace.

Planes that don't contain the origin fail those tests. When $v$ is on such a plane, $- v$
and $O v$ are not on the plane. A plane that misses the origin is not a subspace.

Lines through the origin are also subspaces. When we multiply by 5, or add two
vectors on the line, we stay on the line. But the line must go through $\left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="123" -->
<!-- PageHeader="3.1. Spaces of Vectors" -->

Another subspace is all of $R ^ { 3 } .$ The whole space is a subspace (of itself). Here is a list
of all the possible subspaces of $R ^ { 3 } :$

(L) Any line through $\left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right)$
$\left( R ^ { 3 } \right)$ The whole space

(P) Any plane through $\left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right)$

$\left( Z \right)$ The single vector $\left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right)$

If we try to keep only part of a plane or line, the requirements for a subspace don't hold.
Look at these examples in $R ^ { 2 } .$

Example 1 Keep only the vectors $\left( x , y \right)$ whose components are positive or zero (this is
a quarter-plane). The vector $\left( 2 , 3 \right)$ is included but $\left( - 2 , - 3 \right)$ is not. So rule (ii) is violated
when we try to multiply by $c = - \quad 1 .$ The quarter-plane is not a subspace.

Example 2 Include also the vectors whose components are both negative. Now we have
two quarter-planes. Requirement (ii) is satisfied; we can multiply $b y$ any c. But rule (i)
now fails. The sum of $v = \left( 2 , 3 \right)$ and $w = \left( - 3 , - 2 \right)$ is $\left( - 1 , 1 \right) ,$ which is outside the
quarter-planes. Two quarter-planes don't make a subspace.

Rules (i) and (ii) involve vector addition $v + w$ and multiplication by scalars like $\mathcal{C}$ and
$d .$ The rules can be combined into a single requirement-the rule for subspaces:

A subspace containing v and w must contain all linear combinations cv + dw.

Example 3 Inside the vector space $M$ of all 2 by 2 matrices, here are two subspaces:

(U) All upper triangular matrices
$\left[ \begin{array}{} { a } & { b } \\ { 0 } & { d } \end{array} \right]$
(D) All diagonal matrices
$\left[ \begin{array}{} { a } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { d } \end{array} \right]$

Add any two matrices in $\mathrm { U } ,$ and the sum is in $\mathrm { U } .$ Add diagonal matrices, and the sum is
diagonal. In this case $D$ is also a subspace of $U !$ Of course the zero matrix is in these
subspaces, when $a ,$ $b ,$ and $d$ all equal zero.

To find a smaller subspace of diagonal matrices, we could require $a = d .$ The matrices
are multiples of the identity matrix $I .$ The sum $2 1 + 3 1$ is in this subspace, and so is 3
times 41. The matrices $c /$ form a "line of matrices" inside $M$ and $\mathrm { U }$ and $D .$

Is the matrix $I$ a subspace by itself? Certainly not. Only the zero matrix is. Your mind
will invent more subspaces of 2 by 2 matrices-write them down for Problem 5.


## The Column Space of A

The most important subspaces are tied directly to a matrix $A .$ We are trying to solve
$A x = b .$ If A is not invertible, the system is solvable for some $b$ and not solvable for
other $b .$ We want to describe the good right sides $b$ b-the vectors that can be written as $A$
times some vector $x .$ Those $b ^ { \prime } s$ form the "column space" of A.

Remember that $A x$ is a combination of the columns of $A .$ To get every possible $b ,$ we
use every possible $x .$ So start with the columns of $A ,$ and take all their linear combinations.
This produces the column space of A. It is a vector space made up of column vectors.

$C \left( A \right)$ contains not just the $n$ columns of A, but all their combinations $A x .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="124" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 3. Vector Spaces and Subspaces" -->

DEFINITION The column space consists of all linear combinations of the columns. The
combinations are all possible vectors $A x .$ They fill the column space C (A).

This column space is crucial to the whole book, and here is why. To solve $A x = b$ is
to express $b$ as $a$ combination of the columns. The right side $b$ has to be in the column
space produced by A on the left side, or no solution!

The system $A x = b$ is solvable if and only if b is in the column space of A.

When $b$ is in the column space, it is a combination of the columns. The coefficients in
that combination give us a solution $x$ to the system $A x = b .$

Suppose $A$ is an $m$ by $n$ matrix. Its columns have $m$ components (not n). So the
columns belong to $R ^ { m } .$ The column space of A is $a$ subspace of $\mathrm { R } ^ { m }$ (not $\mathrm { R } ^ { n }$ ). The set
of all column combinations $A x$ satisfies rules (i) and (ii) for $\mathrm { a }$ subspace: When we add
linear combinations or multiply by scalars, we still produce combinations of the columns.
The word "subspace" is justified by taking all linear combinations.

Here is a 3 by 2 matrix $A ,$ whose column space is a subspace of $R ^ { 3 } .$ The column space
of $A$ is a plane in Figure 3.2.


### Example 4

$$A x \text { is } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { x _ { 1 } } \\ { x _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right]$$

ich is X1 4 + x2 3

$b = x _ { 1 } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } \\ { 4 } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right] + x _ { 2 } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } \\ { 3 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right]$

$P l a n e = C \left( A \right) = a l l \quad v e c t o r s \quad A . x$

Figure 3.2: The column space $C \left( A \right)$ is a plane containing the two columns. $A x = b$ is
solvable when $b$ is on that plane. Then $b$ is a combination of the columns.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="125" -->
<!-- PageHeader="3.1. Spaces of Vectors" -->

The column space of all combinations of the two columns fills up a plane in $R ^ { 3 } .$
We drew one particular $b$ (a combination of the columns). This $b = A x$ lies on the plane.
The plane has zero thickness, so most right sides $b$ in $R ^ { 3 }$ are not in the column space. For
most $b$ there is no solution to our 3 equations in 2 unknowns.

Of course $\left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right)$ is in the column space. The plane passes through the origin. There
is certainly $a$ solution to $A x = 0 .$ That solution, always available, is $x =$

To repeat, the attainable right sides $b$ are exactly the vectors in the column space. One
possibility is the first column itself-take $x _ { 1 } = 1$ and $x _ { 2 } = 0 .$ Another combination is the
second column-take $x _ { 1 } = 0$ and $x _ { 2 } = 1 .$ The new level of understanding is to see all
combinations-the whole subspace is generated by those two columns.

Notation The column space of $A$ is denoted by $C \left( A \right) .$ Start with the columns and take all
their linear combinations. We might get the whole $R ^ { m }$ or only a subspace.

Important Instead of columns in $R ^ { m } ,$ we could start with any set $\mathbb{S}$ of vectors in a vector
space $\mathrm { V } .$ To get a subspace SS of $\mathrm { V } ,$ we take all combinations of the vectors in that set:

S = set of vectors in $\mathrm { V }$ (probably not a subspace)

$$S S = a l l \quad c o m b i n a t i o n s \quad o f \quad v e c t o r s \quad i n \quad S$$

SS = allc1v1+ ... +CNUN =the subspace of V "spanned" by $\mathbb{S}$

When $\mathrm { S }$ is the set of columns, SS is the column space. When there is only one nonzero
vector $v$ in $S ,$ the subspace SS is the line through v. Always SS is the smallest subspace
containing S. This is a fundamental way to create subspaces and we will come back to it.

The subspace SS is the "span" of $\mathrm { S } ,$ containing all combinations of vectors in S.

Example 5 Describe the column spaces (they are subspaces of $R ^ { 2 }$ for

$$I = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$$
$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]$$
$$B = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] .$$
and and

Solution The column space of $I$ is the whole space $R ^ { 2 } .$ Every vector is a combination of
the columns of $I .$ In vector space language, $C \left( I \right)$ is $R ^ { 2 } .$

The column space of A is only a line. The second column $\left( 2 , 4 \right)$ is a multiple of the first
column $\left( 1 , 2 \right) .$ Those vectors are different, but our eye is on vector spaces. The column
space contains $\left( 1 , 2 \right)$ and $\left( 2 , 4 \right)$ and all other vectors $\left( c , 2 c \right)$ along that line. The equation
$A x = b$ is only solvable when $b$ is on the line.

For the third matrix (with three columns) the column space $C \left( B \right)$ is all of $R ^ { 2 } .$ Every
$b$ is attainable. The vector $b = \left( 5 , 4 \right)$ is column 2 plus column 3, so $x$ can be $\left( 0 , 1 , 1 \right) .$
The same vector $\left( 5 , 4 \right)$ is also $2 \left( c o l u m n \quad 1 \right) + c o l u m n \quad 3 ,$ so another possible $x$ is $\left( 2 , 0 , 1 \right) .$
This matrix has the same column space as $I$ I-any $b$ is allowed. But now $x$ has extra
components and there are more solutions-more combinations that give $b .$

The next section creates a vector space $N \left( A \right) ,$ to describe all the solutions of $A x = 0 .$
This section created the column space $C \left( A \right) ,$ to describe all the attainable right sides $b .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="126" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 3. Vector Spaces and Subspaces" -->


## REVIEW OF THE KEY IDEAS

1\. $R ^ { n }$ contains all column vectors with $n$ real components.

2\. $M$ (2 by 2 matrices) and $F$ (functions) and $Z$ (zero vector alone) are vector spaces.

3\. A subspace containing $v$ and $w$ must contain all their combinations $c v + d w .$

4\. The combinations of the columns of A form the column space $C \left( A \right) .$ Then the
column space is "spanned" by the columns.

5\. $A x = b$ has a solution exactly when $b$ is in the column space of A.


## WORKED EXAMPLES

3.1 A We are given three different vectors $b _ { 1 } ,$ $b _ { 2 } ,$ $b _ { 3 } .$ Construct a matrix so that the
equations $A x = b _ { 1 }$ and $A x = b _ { 2 }$ are solvable, but $A x = b _ { 3 }$ is not solvable. How can you
decide if this is possible? How could you construct A?

Solution We want to have $b _ { 1 }$ and $b _ { 2 }$ in the column space of A. Then $A x = b _ { 1 }$ and
$A x = b _ { 2 }$ will be solvable. The quickest way is to make $b _ { 1 }$ and $b _ { 2 }$ the two columns of A.
Then the solutions are $x = \left( 1 , 0 \right)$ and $x = \left( 0 , 1 \right) .$

Also, we don't want $A x = b _ { 3 }$ to be solvable. So don't make the column space any
larger! Keeping only the columns of $b _ { 1 }$ and $b _ { 2 } ,$ the question is:

$$A x = \left[ \begin{array}{} { b _ { 1 } } & { b _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { x _ { 1 } } \\ { x _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] = b _ { 3 } \text { solvable? } \quad \text { Is } b _ { 3 } \text { a combination of } b _ { 1 }$$
and $b _ { 2 }$

If the answer is no, we have the desired matrix $A .$ If the answer is yes, then it is not possible
to construct A. When the column space contains $b _ { 1 }$ and $b _ { 2 } ,$ it will have to contain all their
linear combinations. So $b _ { 3 }$ would necessarily be in that column space and $A x = b _ { 3 }$ would
necessarily be solvable.

.1 B Describe a subspace $\mathbb{S}$ of each vector space $\mathrm { V } ,$ and then a subspace SS of $\mathbb{S} .$

V1 = all combinations of $\left( 1 , 1 , 0 , 0 \right)$ and $\left( 1 , 1 , 1 , 0 \right)$ and $\left( 1 , 1 , 1 , 1 \right)$

$V _ { 2 } = a l l$ vectors
$$V _ { 3 } = \text { all symmetric } 2 \text { by } 2 \text { matrices } \left( a \text { subspace of } M \right)$$
$V _ { 4 } = a l$ solutions to the equation $d ^ { 4 } y / d x ^ { 4 } = 0$ (a subspace of F)

Describe each $\mathrm { V }$ two ways: All combinations of .... , all solutions of the equations ... .

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="127" -->
<!-- PageHeader="3.1. Spaces of Vectors" -->

Solution $\mathrm { V } _ { 1 }$ starts with three vectors. A subspace $\mathrm { S }$ comes from all combinations of the
first two vectors $\left( 1 , 1 , 0 , 0 \right)$ and $\left( 1 , 1 , 1 , 0 \right) .$ A subspace SS of $\mathbb{S}$ comes from all multiples
$\left( c , c , 0 , 0 \right)$ of the first vector. So many possibilities.

A subspace $\mathrm { S }$ of $V _ { 2 }$ is the line through $\left( 1 , - 1 , 1 \right) .$ This line is perpendicular to u. The
vector $x = \left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right)$ is in $\mathbb{S}$ and all its multiples $c x$ give the smallest subspace $S S = Z .$

The diagonal matrices are a subspace $\mathrm { S }$ of the symmetric matrices. The multiples $c l$
are a subspace SS of the diagonal matrices.

$V _ { 4 }$ contains all cubic polynomials $y = a + b x + c x ^ { 2 } + d x ^ { 3 } ,$ with $d ^ { 4 } y / d x ^ { 4 } = 0 .$
The quadratic polynomials give a subspace $\mathrm { S } .$ The linear polynomials are one choice of
SS. The constants could be SSS.

In all four parts we could take $S = V$ itself, and $S S = t h e \quad z e r o \quad s u b s p a c e \quad Z .$

Each $\mathrm { V }$ can be described as all combinations of .... and as all solutions $o f \ldots . . :$

V1 = all combinations of the 3 vectors
V1 = all solutions of $v _ { 1 } - v _ { 2 } = 0$
$V _ { 2 } = a l l$ combinations of $\left( 1 , 0 , - 1 \right)$ and $\left( 1 , - 1 , 1 \right)$ are solutions of $u \cdot v = 0 .$
$V _ { 3 } = a l l$ combinations of $\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] , \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] , \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] .$ $V _ { 3 } = a$ solutions $\left[ \begin{array}{} { a } & { b } \\ { c } & { d } \end{array} \right]$ of $b = c$
$V _ { 4 } = a l l$ combinations of 1, $x ,$ $x ^ { 2 } ,$ $x ^ { 3 }$ $V _ { 4 } = a l l$ solutions to $d ^ { 4 } y / d x ^ { 4 } = 0 .$


### Problem Set 3.1

The first problems 1-8 are about vector spaces in general. The vectors in those spaces
are not necessarily column vectors. In the definition of a vector space, vector addition
$x + y$ and scalar multiplication $c x$ must obey the following eight rules:

(1) $x + y = y + x$

(2) $x + \left( y + z \right) = \left( x + y \right) + z$

(3) There is a unique "zero vector" such that $x + 0 = x$ for all $x$

(4) $F o r \quad e a c h \quad x$ there is a unique vector $- x$ such that $x + \left( - x \right) = 0$

(5) 1 times $x$ equals $x$

(6) $\left( c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } \right) x = c _ { 1 } \left( c _ { 2 } x \right)$

(7) $c \left( x + y \right) = c x + c y$

(8) $\left( c _ { 1 } + c _ { 2 } \right) x = c _ { 1 } x + c _ { 2 } x .$

1
Suppose $\left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right) + \left( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } \right)$ is defined to be $\left( x _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } , x _ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } \right) .$ With the usual
multiplication $c x = \left( c x _ { 1 } , c x _ { 2 } \right) ,$ which of the eight conditions are not satisfied?

2
Suppose the multiplication $c x$ is defined to produce $\left( c x _ { 1 } , 0 \right)$ instead of $\left( c x _ { 1 } , c x _ { 2 } \right) .$
With the usual addition in $R ^ { 2 } ,$ are the eight conditions satisfied?

<!-- PageNumber="128" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 3. Vector Spaces and Subspaces" -->

(b) The positive numbers with $x + y$ and $c x$ redefined to equal the usual $x y$ and
$x ^ { c }$ do satisfy the eight rules. Test rule 7 when $c = 3 ,$ $x = 2 ,$ $y = 1 .$ (Then
$x + y = 2$ and $\left. c x = 8 . \right)$ Which number acts as the "zero vector"?

combination $3 \quad f \left( x \right) - \quad 4 g \left( x \right)$ is the function $h \left( x \right) =$

Which rule is broken if multiplying $f \left( x \right)$ by $c$ gives the function $f \left( c x \right) ?$ Keep the
usual addition $f \left( x \right) + g \left( x \right) .$

If the sum of the "vectors" $f \left( x \right)$ and $g \left( x \right)$ is defined to be the function $f \left( g \left( x \right) \right) ,$
then the "zero vector" is $g \left( x \right) = x .$ Keep the usual scalar multiplication $c \quad f \left( x \right)$ and
find two rules that are broken.

Questions 9-18 are about the "subspace requirements": $x + y$ and $c x$ (and then all
linear combinations $\left. c x + d y \right)$ stay in the subspace.

9
One requirement can be met while the other fails. Show this by finding

(a) $\mathrm { A }$ set of vectors in $R ^ { 2 }$ for which $x + y$ stays in the set but $\frac { 1 } { 2 } x$ may be outside.

in the set but $x + y$ may be outside.

10
Which of the following subsets of $R ^ { 3 }$ are actually subspaces?

(a) The plane of vectors $\left( b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , b _ { 3 } \right)$ with $b _ { 1 } = b _ { 2 } .$

(b) The plane of vectors with $b _ { 1 } = 1 .$

(c) The vectors with $b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } b _ { 3 } = 0 .$

(e) All vectors that satisfy $b _ { 1 } + b _ { 2 } + b _ { 3 } = 0 .$

(f) All vectors with $b _ { 1 } \leq b _ { 2 } \leq b _ { 3 } .$

True or false (check addition in each case by an example):

(a) The symmetric matrices in $M$ (with $A ^ { T } = A$ form a subspace.

(b) The skew-symmetric matrices in $M$ (with $A ^ { T } = - A$ form a subspace.

(c) The unsymmetric matrices in $M$ (with $\left. A ^ { T } \neq A \right)$ form a subspace.

Questions 19-27 are about column spaces $C \left( A \right)$ and the equation $A x = b .$

$$C = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]$$

Suppose $\mathbb{S}$ and $\mathrm { T }$ are two subspaces of a vector space $\mathbb{V} .$

(a) Definition: The sum $S + T$ contains all sums $s + t$ of a vector $\mathcal{S}$ in S and a
vector $t$ in $\mathrm { T } .$ Show that $S + T$ satisfies the requirements (addition and scalar
multiplication) for a vector space.

(b) If $\mathrm { S }$ and $\mathrm { T }$ are lines in $R ^ { m } ,$ what is the difference between $S + T$ and $S \cup T ^ { \prime }$
That union contains all vectors from $\mathrm { S }$ or T or both. Explain this statement:
The span of S UT is $\mathrm { S } + \mathrm { T } .$ (Section 3.5 returns to this word "span".)

Show that the matrices A and $\left[ \begin{array}{} { A } & { A B } \end{array} \right]$ (with extra columns) have the same column
space. But find a square matrix with $C \left( A ^ { 2 } \right)$ smaller than $C \left( A \right) .$ Important point:
$A n n$ by $n$ matrix has $C \left( A \right) = R ^ { n }$ exactly when A is an

<!-- PageNumber="132" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 3. Vector Spaces and Subspaces" -->


### 3.2 The Nullspace of A: Solving $A x = 0$

This section is about the subspace containing all solutions to $A x = 0 .$ The $m$ by $n$ matrix
A can be square or rectangular. One immediate solution is $x = 0 .$ For invertible matrices
this is the only solution. For other matrices, not invertible, there are nonzero solutions to
$A x = 0 .$ Each solution $x$ belongs to the nullspace of A.

Elimination will find all solutions and identify this very important subspace.

The nullspace of A consists of all solutions to $A x = 0 .$ These vectors x are in $R ^ { n } .$
The nullspace containing all solutions of $A x = 0$ is denoted by N (A).

Check that the solution vectors form a subspace. Suppose $x$ and $y$ are in the nullspace (this
means $A x = 0$ and $\left. A y = 0 \right) .$ The rules of matrix multiplication give $A \left( x + y \right) = 0 + 0 .$
The rules also give $A \left( c x \right) = c 0 .$ The right sides are still zero. Therefore $x + y$ and $c x$ are
also in the nullspace $N \left( A \right) .$ Since we can add and multiply without leaving the nullspace,
it is a subspace.

To repeat: The solution vectors $x$ have $n$ components. They are vectors in $R ^ { n } ,$ so the
nullspace is a subspace of $R ^ { n } .$ The column space $C \left( A \right)$ is a subspace of $R ^ { m } .$

If the right side b is not zero, the solutions of $A x = b$ do not form a subspace. The
vector $x = 0$ is only a solution if $b = 0 .$ When the set of solutions does not include $x = 0 ,$
it cannot be a subspace. Section 3.4 will show how the solutions to $A x = b$ (if there are
any solutions) are shifted away from the origin by one particular solution.

Example 1 $x + 2 y + 3 z = 0$ comes from the 1 by 3 matrix $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] .$ This
equation $A x = 0$ produces a plane through the origin $\left( 0 , 0 , 0 \right) .$ The plane is a subspace of
$R ^ { 3 } .$ It is the nullspace of $A .$

The solutions to $x + 2 y + 3 z = 6$ also form a plane, but not a subspace.

Example 2 Describe the nullspace of $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right]$
This matrix is singular !

Solution
Apply elimination to the linear equations $A x = 0 :$

$$\begin{array}{} { x _ { 1 } + 2 x _ { 2 } = 0 } & { x _ { 1 } + 2 x _ { 2 } = 0 } \\ { 3 x _ { 1 } + 6 x _ { 2 } = 0 } & { 0 = 0 } \end{array}$$

There is really only one equation. The second equation is the first equation multiplied by
3. In the row picture, the line $x _ { 1 } + 2 x _ { 2 } = 0$ is the same as the line $3 x _ { 1 } + 6 x _ { 2 } = 0 .$ That
line is the nullspace $N \left( A \right) .$ It contains all solutions $\left( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } \right) .$

To describe this line of solutions, here is an efficient way. Choose one point on the line
(one "special solution"). Then all points on the line are multiples of this one. We choose
the second component to be $x _ { 2 } = 1$ (a special choice). From the equation $x _ { 1 } + 2 x _ { 2 } = 0 ,$
the first component must be $x _ { 1 } = - 2 .$ The special solution $\mathcal{S}$ is $\left( - 2 , 1 \right) :$

Special
solution

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right] \text { contans of } x = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 } & { - 2 } \\ { 3 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="133" -->
<!-- PageHeader="3.2. The Nullspace of $A :$ Solving $A x = 0$" -->

This is the best way to describe the nullspace, by computing special solutions to $A x = 0 .$
This example has one special solution and the nullspace is a line.

The nullspace consists of all combinations of the special solutions.

The plane $x + 2 y + 3 z = 0$ in Example 1 had two special solutions:

$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { x } \\ { y } \\ { z } \end{array} \right] = 0$
has the special solutions $s s _ { 1 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 2 } \\ { 1 } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right]$

and
$s _ { 2 } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { - 3 } \\ { 0 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right]$

Those vectors $s _ { 1 }$ and $s _ { 2 }$ lie on the plane $x + 2 y + 3 z = 0 ,$ which is the nullspace of
$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] .$ All vectors on the plane are combinations of $\mathcal{S} 1$ and $\mathcal{S} _ { 2 } .$

Notice what is special about $\mathcal{S} _ { 1 }$ and $\mathcal{S} _ { 2 } .$ They have ones and zeros in the last two
components. Those components are $e ^ { x }$ and we choose them specially. Then the first
components -2 and $- 3$ are determined by the equation $A x = 0 .$

The first column of $A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]$ ]contains the $p i v o t ,$ so the first component of $x$ is
not free. The free components correspond to columns without pivots. This description of
special solutions will be completed after one more example.

The special choice (one or zero) is only for the free variables.

Example 3 Describe the nullspaces of these three matrices $A ,$ $B ,$ $C :$

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right] \quad B = \left[ \begin{array}{} { A } \\ { 2 } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 8 } \\ { 2 } & { 4 } \\ { 6 } & { 1 6 } \end{array} \right] \quad C = \left[ \begin{array}{} { A } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 2 } & { 4 } \\ { 3 } & { 8 } & { 1 6 } \end{array} \right]$$

Solution The equation $A x = 0$ has only the zero solution $x = 0 .$ The nullspace is $Z .$
It contains only the single point $x = 0$ in $R ^ { 2 } .$ This comes from elimination:

$$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { x _ { 1 } } \\ { x _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] \text { yields } \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { x _ { 1 } } \\ { x _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } \\ { 0 } \end{array} \right] \text { and } \left[ \begin{array}{} { x _ { 1 } } & { = } & { 0 } \\ { x _ { 2 } } & { = } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]$$

A is invertible. There are no special solutions. All columns of this A have pivots.

The rectangular matrix $B$ has the same nullspace Z. The first two equations in $B x = 0$
again require $x = 0 .$ The last two equations would also force $x = 0 .$ When we add
extra equations, the nullspace certainly cannot become larger. The extra rows impose more
conditions on the vectors $x$ in the nullspace.

The rectangular matrix $C$ is different. It has extra columns instead of extra rows. The
solution vector $x$ has four components. Elimination will produce pivots in the first two
columns of $C ,$ but the last two columns are "free". They don't have pivots:

$$C = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 2 } & { 4 } \\ { 3 } & { 8 } & { 6 } & { 1 6 } \end{array} \right] \text { becomes } U = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 2 } & { 2 } & { 4 } \\ { 0 } & { 2 } & { 0 } & { 4 } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$$

pivot columns free columns

For the free variables $x _ { 3 }$ and $x _ { 4 } ,$ we make special choices of ones and zeros. First $x _ { 3 } = 1 ,$
$x _ { 4 } = 0$ and second $x _ { 3 } = 0 ,$ $x _ { 4 } = 1$ 1. The pivot variables $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 }$ are determined by the

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="134" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 3. Vector Spaces and Subspaces" -->

equation $U x = 0 .$ We get two special solutions in the nullspace of $C$ (which is also the
nullspace of $\left. U \right) .$ The special solutions are $\mathcal{S} _ { 1 }$ and $s _ { 2 } :$

$1 =

$$\begin{array}{} { - 2 } \\ { 0 } \\ { 1 } \\ { 0 } \end{array}$$

One more comment to anticipate what is coming soon. Elimination will not stop at the
upper triangular $U !$ We can continue to make this matrix simpler, in two ways:

1\. Produce zeros above the pivots, by eliminating upward.

2\. Produce ones in the pivots, by dividing the whole row by its pivot.

Those steps don't change the zero vector on the right side of the equation. The nullspace
stays the same. This nullspace becomes easiest to see when we reach the reduced row
echelon form $R .$ It has $I$ in the pivot columns:


<figure>

Reduced
form $R$
0- 0 2 0 4
1 2 4

1020
1 1
1

becomes R =
0102

now the pivot columns contain $I$

</figure>


I subtracted row 2 of $U$ from row 1, and then multiplied row 2 by $\frac { 1 } { 2 } .$ The original two
equations have simplified to $x _ { 1 } + 2 x _ { 3 } = 0$ and $x _ { 2 } + 2 x _ { 4 } = 0 .$

The first special solution is still $s _ { 1 } = \left( - 2 , 0 , 1 , 0 \right) ,$ and $s _ { 2 }$ is also unchanged. Special
solutions are much easier to find from the reduced system $R x = 0 .$

Before moving to $m$ by $n$ matrices A and their nullspaces $N \left( A \right)$ and special solutions,
allow me to repeat one comment. For many matrices, the only solution to $A x = 0$ is $x = 0 .$
Their nullspaces $N \left( A \right) = Z$ contain only that zero vector. The only combination of the
columns that produces $b = 0$ is then the "zero combination" or "trivial combination".
The solution is trivial (just $\left. x = 0 \right)$ but the idea is not trivial.

This case of a zero nullspace Z is of the greatest importance. It says that the columns
of A are independent. No combination of columns gives the zero vector (except the zero
combination). All columns have pivots, and no columns are free. You will see this idea of
independence again . . .


## Solving $A x = 0$ by Elimination

This is important. $A$ is rectangular and we still use elimination. We solve $m$ equations in
$n$ unknowns when $b = 0 .$ After A is simplified by row operations, we read off the solution
(or solutions). Remember the two stages (forward and back) in solving $A x = 0 :$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="135" -->
<!-- PageHeader="3.2. The Nullspace of $A :$ Solving $A x = 0$" -->

1\. Forward elimination takes $A$ to a triangular $U$ (or its reduced form $\left. R \right) .$

2\. Back substitution in $U x = 0$ or $R x = 0$ produces $x .$

You will notice a difference in back substitution, when $A$ and $U$ have fewer than $n$
pivots. We are allowing all matrices in this chapter, not just the nice ones (which are
square matrices with inverses).

Pivots are still nonzero. The columns below the pivots are still zero. But it might
happen that a column has no pivot. That free column doesn't stop the calculation. Go on
to the next column. The first example is a 3 by 4 matrix with two pivots:

$$A = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 2 } & { 8 } & { 1 0 } \\ { 3 } & { 3 } & { 1 0 } & { 1 3 } \end{array} \right] .$$

Certainly $a _ { 1 1 } = 1$ is the first pivot. Clear out the 2 and 3 below that pivot:

$$A \rightarrow \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 4 } & { 4 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 4 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] \quad \left( \text { subtract } 2 \times r o w 1 \right)$$

The second column has a zero in the pivot position. We look below the zero for a nonzero
entry, ready to do a row exchange. The entry below that position is also zero. Elimination
can do nothing with the second column. This signals trouble, which we expect anyway for
a rectangular matrix. There is no reason to quit, and we go on to the third column.

The second pivot is 4 (but it is in the third column). Subtracting row 2 from row 3 clears
out that column below the pivot. The pivot columns are 1 and 3:

Triangular $U$ : V = 0.0 4.4.
0 0 0.0
1 1 2 3] Only two pivots
The last equation
became $0 = 0$

The fourth column also has a zero in the pivot position-but nothing can be done. There
is no row below it to exchange, and forward elimination is complete. The matrix has three
rows, four columns, and only two pivots. The original $A x = 0$ seemed to involve three
different equations, but the third equation is the sum of the first two. It is automatically
satisfied $\left( 0 = 0 \right)$ when the first two equations are satisfied. Elimination reveals the inner
truth about a system of equations. Soon we push on from $U$ to $R .$

Now comes back substitution, to find all solutions to $U x = 0 .$ With four unknowns
and only two pivots, there are many solutions. The question is how to write them all down.
A good method is to separate the pivot variables from the free variables.

P The pivot variables are $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 3 } .$
Columns 1 and 3 contain pivots.

F
The free variables are $x _ { 2 }$ and $x _ { 4 } .$
Columns 2 and 4 have no pivots.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="136" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 3. Vector Spaces and Subspaces" -->

The free variables $x _ { 2 }$ and $x _ { 4 }$ can be given any values whatsoever. Then back substitution
finds the pivot variables $x _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 3 } .$ (In Chapter 2 no variables were free. When A is
invertible, all variables are pivot variables.) The simplest choices for the free variables are
ones and zeros. Those choices give the special solutions.

Special solutions to $x _ { 1 } + x _ { 2 } + 2 x _ { 3 } + 3 x _ { 4 } = 0$ and $4 x _ { 3 } + 4 x _ { 4 } = 0$

· Set $x _ { 2 } = 1$ and $x _ { 4 } = 0 .$ By back substitution $x _ { 3 } = 0 .$ Then $x _ { 1 } = - 1 .$

· Set $x _ { 2 } = 0$ and $x _ { 4 } = 1 .$ By back substitution $x _ { 3 } = - 1 .$ Then $x _ { 1 } = - 1 .$

These special solutions solve $U x = 0$ and therefore $A x = 0 .$ They are in the nullspace.
The good thing is that every solution is a combination of the special solutions.


<figure>

Complete solution
1
$t o \quad A x = 0$
