## Conceptual Questions for Review


### Chapter 1

1.1 Which vectors are linear combinations of $v = \left( 3 , 1 \right)$ and $w = \left( 4 , 3 \right) ?$

1.2 Compare the dot product of $v = \left( 3 , 1 \right)$ and $w = \left( 4 , 3 \right)$ to the product of their lengths.
Which is larger? Whose inequality?

1.3 What is the cosine of the angle between $v$ and $w$ in Question 1.2? What is the cosine
of the angle between the x-axis and v?


### Chapter 2

2.1 Multiplying a matrix $A$ times the column vector $x = \left( 2 , - 1 \right)$ gives what combination
of the columns of A? How many rows and columns in A?

2.2 If $A x = b$ then the vector $b$ is a linear combination of what vectors from the matrix
A? In vector space language, $b$ lies in the
space of A.

2.3 If $A$ is the 2 by 2 matrix [{ } ] what are its pivots?

2.4 If A is the matrix
$\left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$
how does elimination proceed? What permutation matrix $P$
is involved?
.

2.5 If A is the matrix $\left[ \begin{array}{} { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 6 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]$
find $b$ and $c$ so that $A x = b$ has no solution and $A x = c$ has
a solution.

2.6 What 3 by 3 matrix $L$ adds 5 times row 2 to row 3 and then adds 2 times row $1 \quad t o$
row 2, when it multiplies a matrix with three rows?

2.7 What 3 by 3 matrix $E$ subtracts 2 times row 1 from row 2 and then subtracts 5 times
row 2 from row 3? How is $E$ related to $L$ in Question 2.6?

2.8 If A is 4 by 3 and $B$ is 3 by 7, how many row times column products go into $A B ?$
How many column times row products go into $A B ?$ How many separate small mul-
tiplications are involved (the same for both)?

2.10 How can you find the inverse of A by working with $\left[ \begin{array}{} { A } & { I } \end{array} \right]$ ]? If you solve the $n$
equations $A x = c o l u m n s \quad o f \quad I$ then the solutions $x$ are columns of
·

2.11 How does elimination decide whether a square matrix A is invertible?

2.12 Suppose elimination takes A to $U$ (upper triangular) by row operations with the
multipliers in L (lower triangular). Why does the last row of A agree with the last
row of $L$ times $U ?$

2.13 What is the factorization (from elimination with possible row exchanges) of any
square invertible matrix?

2.14 What is the transpose of the inverse of $A B ?$

2.15 How do you know that the inverse of a permutation matrix is a permutation matrix?
How is it related to the transpose?


### Chapter 3

3.1 What is the column space of an invertible $n$ by $n$ matrix? What is the nullspace of
that matrix?

3.2 If every column of $A$ is a multiple of the first column, what is the column space of
$A ?$

3.3 What are the two requirements for a set of vectors in $R ^ { n }$ to be a subspace?

3.4 If the row reduced form $R$ of a matrix A begins with a row of ones, how do you know
that the other rows of $R$ are zero and what is the nullspace?

3.5 Suppose the nullspace of $A$ contains only the zero vector. What can you say about
solutions to $A x = b ?$

3.6 From the row reduced form $R ,$ how would you decide the rank of A?

3.7 Suppose column 4 of A is the sum of columns 1, 2, and 3. Find a vector in the
nullspace.

3.9 If $A x = b$ has exactly one solution for every $b ,$ what can you say $a b o u t \quad A ?$

3.11 What is the dimension of the space of 4 by 4 symmetric matrices?

3.12 Describe the meaning of basis and dimension of a vector space.

### Chapter 5

5.1 What is the determinant of the matrix $- \quad I$ ?

5.2 Explain how the determinant is a linear function of the first row.

5.3 How do you know that det $A ^ { - 1 } = 1 / \mathrm { d e t } A$

5.4 If the pivots of $A$ (with no row exchanges) are 2, 6, 6, what submatrices of A have
known determinants?

5.5 Suppose the first row of $A$ is 0, 0, 0, 3. What does the "big formula" for the determi-
nant of $A$ reduce to in this case?

5.6 Is the ordering $\left( 2 , 5 , 3 , 4 , 1 \right)$ even or odd? What permutation matrix has what deter-
minant, from your answer?

5.7 What is the cofactor $C _ { 2 3 }$ in the 3 by 3 elimination matrix $E$ that subtracts 4 times
row 1 from row 2? What entry of $E ^ { - 1 }$ is revealed?

5.8 Explain the meaning of the cofactor formula for det $A$ using column 1.

5.9 How does Cramer's Rule give the first component in the solution to $I \quad x = b ?$

5.10 If I combine the entries in row 2 with the cofactors from row 1, why is $a _ { 2 1 } C _ { 1 1 } +$
$a _ { 2 2 } C _ { 1 2 } + a _ { 2 3 } C _ { 1 3 }$ automatically zero?

5.11 What is the connection between determinants and volumes?

5.12 Find the cross product of $u = \left( 0 , 0 , 1 \right)$ and $v = \left( 0 , 1 , 0 \right)$ and its direction.

5.13 If A is $n$ by $n ,$ why is $\mathrm { d e t } \left( A - \lambda I \right)$ a polynomial in $\lambda$ of degree $n$ n?


### Chapter 6

6.1 What equation gives the eigenvalues of A without involving the eigenvectors? How
would you then find the eigenvectors?

6.2 If A is singular what does this say about its eigenvalues?

6.3 If $A$ times A equals $4 A ,$ what numbers can be eigenvalues of A?

6.4 Find a real matrix that has no real eigenvalues or eigenvectors.

6.5 How can you find the sum and product of the eigenvalues directly from $A ?$

6.6 What are the eigenvalues of the rank one matrix [ 1 2 1]T[ 1 1 1]?

6.7 Explain the diagonalization formula $A = S A S ^ { - 1 } .$ Why is it true and when is it true?

6.10 How do the eigenvectors of A help to solve $d u / d t = A u ?$

6.11 How do the eigenvectors of A help to solve $u _ { k + 1 } = A u _ { k }$

6.12 Define the matrix exponential $e ^ { A }$ and its inverse and its square.

6.13 If $A$ is symmetric, what is special about its eigenvectors? Do any other matrices have

6.14 What is the diagonalization formula when $A$ is symmetric?

6.15 What does it mean to say that A is positive definite?

6.17 If A is positive definite describe the surface $x ^ { T } A x = 1 \text { in } R ^ { n } .$

6.18 What does it mean for A and $B$ to be similar? What is sure to be the same for A and
$B ?$

6.19 The 3 by 3 matrix with ones for $i \geq j$ has what Jordan form?

6.20 The SVD expresses A as a product of what three types of matrices?

6.21 How is the SVD for A linked to $A ^ { T } A$


### Chapter 7

7.1 Define a linear transformation from $R ^ { 3 }$ to $R ^ { 2 }$ and give one example.

7.2 If the upper middle house on the cover of the book is the original, find something
nonlinear in the transformations of the other eight houses.

7.3 If a linear transformation takes every vector in the input basis into the next basis
vector (and the last into zero), what is its matrix?

7.4 Suppose we change from the standard basis (the columns of $\left. I \right)$ to the basis given by
the columns of A (invertible matrix). What is the change of basis matrix $M ?$

7.5 Suppose our new basis is formed from the eigenvectors of a matrix $A .$ What matrix
represents $A$ in this new basis?

succeeds (what conditions on $\left. A ? \right) .$

## GLOSSARY: A DICTIONARY FOR LINEAR ALGEBRA

Adjacency matrix of a graph. Square matrix with $a _ { i j } = 1$ when there is an edge from
node $i$ to node $j$ ; otherwise $a _ { i j } = 0 .$ $A = A ^ { T }$ when edges go both ways (undirected).
Affine transformation $T v = A v + v _ { 0 }$ = linear transformation plus shift.

Associative Law $\left( A B \right) C = A \left( B C \right) .$ Parentheses can be removed to leave $A B C .$

Augmented matrix $\left[ \begin{array}{} { A } & { b } \end{array} \right] .$ $A x = b$ is solvable when $b \quad i s \quad i n$ the column space of A; then
$\left[ \begin{array}{} { A } & { b } \end{array} \right]$ has the same rank as A. Elimination on $\left[ \begin{array}{} { A } & { b } \end{array} \right]$ equations correct.

Back substitution. Upper triangular systems are solved in reverse order $x _ { n }$ to $x _ { 1 } .$

Basis for $V .$ Independent vectors $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { d }$ whose linear combinations give each vector
in $V$ as $v = c _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } + \ldots + c _ { d } v _ { d } .$ $V$ has many bases, each basis gives unique c's.
A vector space has many bases!

Big formula for $n$ by $n$ determinants. $\mathrm { D e t } \left( A \right)$ is a sum of $n !$ terms. For each term:
Multiply one entry from each row and column of A: rows in order $1 , \ldots , n$ and
column order given by a permutation $P .$ Each of the $n !$ $P ^ { \prime } s \text { has a } + o r - s i g n .$

Block matrix. A matrix can be partitioned into matrix blocks, by cuts between rows and/or
between columns. Block multiplication of $A \quad B$ is allowed if the block shapes permit.

Cayley-Hamilton Theorem. $p \left( A \right) = \mathrm { d e t } \left( A - \lambda I \right)$ has $p \left( A \right) = z e r o \quad m a t r i x .$

Change of basis matrix $M .$ The old basis vectors $v _ { j }$ are combinations $\sum m _ { i j } w _ { i }$ of the
new basis vectors. The coordinates of $c _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } + \cdots + c _ { n } v _ { n } = d _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + \cdots + d _ { n } w _ { n }$ are
related by $d = M c .$ $\left( F o r n = 2 s e t v _ { 1 } = m _ { 1 1 } w _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 1 } w _ { 2 } \right. ,$ $v _ { 2 } = m _ { 1 2 } w _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 2 } w _ { 2 }$
Characteristic equation $\mathrm { d e t } \left( A - \lambda I \right) = 0 .$ The $n$ roots are the eigenvalues of A.

Cholesky factorization $A = C ^ { T } C = \left( L \sqrt { D } \right) \left( L \sqrt { D } \right) ^ { T }$ for positive definite A.

Circulant matrix $C .$ Constant diagonals wrap around as in cyclic shift $S .$ Every circulant
is $c _ { 0 } I + c _ { 1 } S + \cdots + c _ { n - 1 } S ^ { n - 1 } .$ $C x = c o n v o l u t i o n \quad c * x .$ Eigenvectors in $F .$

Cofactor $C _ { i j } .$ Remove row $i$ and column $j ;$ multiply the determinant by $\left( - 1 \right) ^ { i + j } .$

Column picture of $A x = b .$ The vector b becomes a combination of the columns of $A .$
The system is solvable only when $b \quad i s \quad i n$ the column space $C \left( A \right) .$

Column space $C \left( A \right) = s p a c e$ of all combinations of the columns of $A .$

Commuting matrices $A B = B A .$ If diagonalizable, they share $n$ eigenvectors.

Companion matrix. Put $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { n }$ in row $n$ and put $n \quad - \quad 1$ ones just above the main
diagonal. Then $\mathrm { d e t } \left( A - \lambda I \right) = \pm \left( c _ { 1 } + c _ { 2 } \lambda + c _ { 3 } \lambda ^ { 2 } + \cdots + c _ { n } \lambda ^ { n - 1 } - \lambda ^ { n } \right) .$

Complete solution $x = x _ { p } + x _ { n }$ to $A x = b .$ (Particular $\left. x _ { p } \right) + \left( x _ { n } \right.$ in nullspace).

<!-- PageNumber="557" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="558" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Glossary" -->

Complex conjugate $\bar { z } = a - i b$ for any complex number $z = a + i b .$ Then $z \bar { z } = | z | ^ { 2 } .$
Condition number $\mathrm { c o n d } \left( A \right) = c \left( A \right) = \| A \| \| A ^ { - 1 } \| = \sigma _ { \max } / \sigma _ { \min } .$ In $A x = b ,$ the
relative change $\| 8 x \| / \| x \|$ is less than $\mathrm { c o n d } \left( A \right)$ times the relative change $\| 8 b \| / \| b \| .$
Condition numbers measure the sensitivity of the output to change in the input.

Conjugate Gradient Method. $\mathrm { A }$ sequence of steps (end of Chapter 9) to solve positive
definite $A x = b$ by minimizing $\frac { 1 } { 2 } x ^ { T } A x - x ^ { T } b$ over growing Krylov subspaces.

Covariance matrix $\Sigma .$ When random variables $x _ { i }$ have $m e a n = a v e r a g e \quad v a l u e = 0 ,$ their
covariances $\Sigma _ { i j }$ are the averages of $x _ { i } x _ { j } .$ With means $\bar { x } _ { i } ,$ the matrix $\Sigma = \mathrm { m e a n }$ of
$\left( x - \pi \right) \left( x - x \right) ^ { T }$ is positive (semi)definite; $\Sigma$ is diagonal if the $x _ { i }$ are independent.
Cramer's Rule for $A x = b .$ $B _ { j }$ has $b$ replacing column $j$ of A; $x _ { j } = \det B _ { j } / \det A$
Cross product $u \times v$ in $R ^ { 3 } .$ Vector perpendicular to $u$ and $v ,$ length $| | u | | | v | | \sin \theta | = \mathrm { a r e a }$
of parallelogram, $u \times v =$ of $\left[ i \quad j \quad k ; u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } u _ { 3 } ; v _ { 1 } v _ { 2 } v _ { 3 } \right]$ ].
Cyclic shift $S .$ Permutation with $s _ { 2 1 } = 1 ,$ $s _ { 3 2 } = 1 , \ldots ,$ finally $s _ { 1 n } = 1 .$ Its eigenvalues
are the nth roots $e ^ { 2 \pi i k / n }$ of 1; eigenvectors are columns of the Fourier matrix $F .$
Determinant $| A | = \mathrm { d e t } \left( A \right) .$ Defined by det $I =$ 1, sign reversal for row exchange, and
linearity in each row. Then $| A | = 0$ when $A$ is singular. Also $\left. | A B \right] = | A | B |$ and
$| A ^ { - 1 } | = 1 / | A |$ and $| A ^ { T } | = | A | .$ The big formula for $\mathrm { d e t } \left( A \right)$ has a sum of $n !$ terms,
the cofactor formula uses determinants of size $n \quad - \quad 1 ,$ volume of $b o x = | d e t \left( A \right) | .$
Diagonal matrix $D .$ $d _ { i j } = 0$ if $i \neq j .$ Block-diagonal: zero outside square blocks $D _ { i i }$ .
Diagonalizable matrix A. Must have $n$ independent eigenvectors (in the columns of $S ;$
automatic with $n$ different eigenvalues). Then $S ^ { - 1 } A S = \Lambda = \text { eigenvalue matrix } .$

Diagonalization $\Lambda = S ^ { - 1 } A S .$ $\Lambda = \mathrm { e i g e n v a }$ matrix and $S = e i g e n v e c t o r \quad m a t r i x \quad o f \quad A .$
A must have $n$ independent eigenvectors to make $\mathcal{S}$ invertible. All $A ^ { k } = S \Lambda ^ { k } S ^ { - 1 } .$
Dimension of vector space $\dim \left( V \right) = \text { number of }$ vectors in any basis for $V$ .

Distributive Law $A \left( B + C \right) = A B + A C .$ Add then multiply, or multiply then add.

Dot product = Inner product $x ^ { T } y = x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdots + x _ { n } y _ { n } .$ Complex dot product is $x ^ { T } y .$
Perpendicular vectors have $x ^ { T } y = 0 .$ (AB)ij = (row i of A)™(column j of B).

Echelon matrix $U .$ The first nonzero entry (the pivot) in each row comes in a later column
than the pivot in the previous row. All zero rows come last.

Eigenvalue $\lambda$ and eigenvector x. $A x = \lambda x$ with $x \neq 0$ so $\mathrm { d e t } \left( A - \lambda I \right) = 0 .$

Elimination. A sequence of row operations that reduces A to an upper triangular $U$ or
to the reduced form $R = \mathrm { r r e f } \left( A \right) .$ Then $A = L U$ with multipliers $\ell _ { i j }$ in L, or
$P A = L U$ with row exchanges in $P ,$ or $E A = R$ with an invertible $E .$

Elimination matrix = Elementary matrix $E _ { i j } .$ The identity matrix with an extra $- \ell _ { i j }$
in the i, $j$ entry $\left( i \neq j \right) .$ Then $E _ { i j } A$ subtracts $\ell _ { i j }$ times row $j$ of A from row i.

Ellipse (or ellipsoid) $x ^ { T } A x = 1$ 1. $A$ must be positive definite; the axes of the ellipse are
eigenvectors of $A ,$ with lengths $1 / \sqrt { 2 } .$ (For $\| x \| = 1$ the vectors $y = A x$ lie on the
ellipse $\| A ^ { - 1 } y \| ^ { 2 } = y ^ { T } \left( A A ^ { T } \right) ^ { - 1 } y = 1$ displayed by eigshow; axis lengths $\sigma _ { i } .$ )

Exponential $e ^ { A t } = I + A t + \left( A t \right) ^ { 2 } / 2 ! + \cdots .$ has derivative $A e ^ { A t }$ ; $e ^ { A t } u \left( 0 \right)$ solves $u ^ { \prime } = A u .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="559" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Glossary" -->

Factorization $A = L U .$ If elimination takes $A$ to $U$ without row exchanges, then the
lower triangular $L$ with multipliers $\ell _ { i j }$ (and $\ell _ { i i } = 1$ brings $U$ back to $A .$

Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). A factorization of the Fourier matrix $F _ { n }$ into $\ell = \log _ { 2 } n$
matrices $S _ { i }$ times a permutation. Each $S _ { i }$ needs only $n / 2$ multiplications, so $F _ { n } x$
and $F _ { n } ^ { - 1 } c$ can be computed with $n \ell / 2$ multiplications. Revolutionary.

Fibonacci numbers $0 , 1 , 1 , 2 , 3 , 5 , \ldots .$ satisfy $F _ { n } = F _ { n - 1 } + F _ { n - 2 } = \left( \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { n } - \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { n } \right) / \left( \lambda _ { 1 } - \lambda _ { 2 } \right) .$
Growth rate $A _ { 1 } = \left( 1 + \sqrt { 5 } \right) / 2$ is the largest eigenvalue of the Fibonacci matrix $\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] .$
Four Fundamental Subspaces $C \left( A \right) ,$ $N \left( A \right) ,$ $C \left( A ^ { T } \right) ,$ $N \left( A ^ { T } \right) .$ Use $\frac { 1 } { A }$ for complex $A .$

Fourier matrix $F .$ Entries $F _ { j k } = e ^ { 2 \pi i j k / n }$ give orthogonal columns $\overrightarrow { F } ^ { \mathrm { T } } F = n I .$ Then
$y = F c$ is the (inverse) Discrete Fourier Transform $y _ { j } = \sum c _ { k } e ^ { 2 \pi i j k / n } .$

Free columns of A. Columns without pivots; these are combinations of earlier columns.

Free variable $x _ { i } .$ Column $i$ has no pivot in elimination. We can give the $n \quad - \quad r$ free
variables any values, then $A x = b$ determines the $r$ pivot variables (if solvable!).

Full column rank $r = n .$ Independent columns, $N \left( A \right) = 0 ,$ no free variables.

Full row rank $r = m .$ Independent rows, at least one solution to $A x = b ,$ column space
is all of $R ^ { m } .$ Full rank means full column rank or full row rank.

Fundamental Theorem. The nullspace $N \left( A \right)$ and row space $C \left( A ^ { T } \right)$ are orthogonal com-
plements in R"(perpendicular from $A x = 0$ with dimensions $r$ and $\left. n \quad - \quad r \right) .$ Applied
to $A ^ { T } ,$ the column space $C \left( A \right)$ is the orthogonal complement of $N \left( A ^ { T } \right)$ in $\mathrm { R } ^ { m } .$

Gauss-Jordan method. Invert A by row operations on $\left[ A \quad I \right]$ to reach $\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { A ^ { - 1 } } \end{array} \right] .$

Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization $A = Q R .$ Independent columns in $A ,$ orthonormal
columns in $Q .$ Each column $q _ { j }$ of $Q$ is a combination of the first $j$ columns of A
(and conversely, so $R$ is upper triangular). Convention: $\mathrm { d i a g } \left( R \right) > 0 .$

Graph $G .$ Set of $n$ nodes connected pairwise by $m$ edges. A complete graph has all
$n \left( n \quad - \quad 1 \right) / 2$ edges between nodes. A tree has only $n \quad - \quad 1$ edges and no closed loops.

Hankel matrix $H .$ Constant along each antidiagonal; $h _ { i j }$ depends on $i + j$ .

Hermitian matrix $A ^ { H } = \bar { A } ^ { T } = A .$ Complex analog $a _ { j i } = a _ { i j }$ of a symmetric matrix.
Hessenberg matrix $H .$ Triangular matrix with one extra nonzero adjacent diagonal.

Hilbert matrix $h i l b \left( n \right) .$ Entries $H _ { i j } = 1 / \left( i + j - 1 \right) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } x ^ { i - 1 } x ^ { j - 1 } d x .$ Positive definite
but extremely small $\lambda _ { \min }$ and large condition number: $H$ is ill-conditioned.

Hypercube matrix $P _ { L } ^ { 2 } .$ Row $n + 1$ counts corners, edges, faces,. . . of a cube in $R ^ { n } .$
Identity matrix $I$ (or $I _ { n } .$ Diagonal $e n t r i e s = 1 ,$ off-diagonal entries $= 0 .$

Incidence matrix of a directed graph. The $m$ by $n$ edge-node incidence matrix has a
row for each edge (node $i$ to node $\left. j \right) ,$ with entries -1 and 1 in columns $i$ and $j$ .

Indefinite matrix. A symmetric matrix with eigenvalues of both signs (+ and -).

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="560" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Glossary" -->

Independent vectors $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { k } .$ No combination $c _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } + \cdots + c _ { k } v _ { k } = \text { zero } v$ vector
unless all $c _ { i } = 0 .$ If the v's are the columns of $A ,$ the only solution to $A x = 0$ is
$x = 0 .$

Inverse matrix $A ^ { - 1 } .$ Square matrix with $A ^ { - 1 } A = I$ and $A A ^ { - 1 } = I .$ No inverse if
det $A = 0$ and $r a n k \left( A \right) < n$ and $A x = 0$ for a nonzero vector x. The inverses of $A B$
and $A ^ { T }$ are $B ^ { - 1 } A ^ { - 1 }$ and $\left( A ^ { - 1 } \right) ^ { T } .$ Cofactor formula $\left( A ^ { - 1 } \right) _ { i j } = C _ { j i } / \det A .$

Iterative method. A sequence of steps intended to approach the desired solution.

Jordan form $J = M ^ { - 1 } A M .$ If A has $\mathcal{S}$ independent eigenvectors, its "generalized"
eigenvector matrix $M$ gives $J = \mathrm { d i a g } \left( J _ { 1 } , \ldots , J _ { s } \right) .$ The block $J _ { k }$ is $\lambda _ { k } I _ { k } + N _ { k }$ where
$N _ { k }$ has 1's on diagonal 1. Each block has one eigenvalue $\lambda _ { k }$ and one eigenvector.

Kirchhoff's Laws. Current Law: net current (in minus out) is zero at each node. Voltage
Law: Potential differences (voltage drops) add to zero around any closed loop.

Kronecker product (tensor product) $A \otimes B .$ Blocks $a _ { i j } B ,$ eigenvalues $\lambda _ { p } \left( A \right) \lambda _ { q } \left( B \right) .$
☒

Krylov subspace $K _ { j } \left( A , b \right) .$ The subspace spanned by $b , A b , \ldots , A ^ { j - 1 } b .$ Numerical
methods approximate $A ^ { - 1 } b$ by $x _ { j }$ with residual $b - A x _ { j }$ in this subspace. A good
basis for $K _ { j }$ requires only multiplication by A at each step.

Least squares solution $\widehat { x } .$ The vector $\widehat { x }$ that minimizes the error $\| e \| ^ { 2 }$ solves $A ^ { T } A \widehat { x } =$
$A ^ { T } b .$ Then $e = b - A \widehat { x }$ is orthogonal to all columns of A.

Left inverse $A ^ { + } .$ If A has full column rank $n ,$ then $A ^ { + } = \left( A ^ { T } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { T }$ has $A ^ { + } A = I _ { n } .$
Left nullspace $N \left( A ^ { T } \right) .$ Nullspace of $A ^ { T } = { } ^ { 4 } \text { eft }$ nullspace" of $A$ because $y ^ { T } A = 0 ^ { T } .$
Length $\| x \| .$ Square root of $x ^ { T } x$ (Pythagoras in $n$ dimensions).

Linear combination $c v + d w$ or $\sum c _ { j } v _ { j } .$ Vector addition and scalar multiplication.

Linear transformation $T .$ Each vector $v$ in the input space transforms to $T \left( v \right)$ in the
output space, and linearity requires $T \left( c v + d w \right) = c T \left( v \right) + d \quad T \left( w \right) .$ Examples:
Matrix multiplication $A v ,$ differentiation and integration in function space.

Linearly dependent $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { n } .$ A combination other than all $c _ { i } = 0$ gives $\sum c _ { i } v _ { i } = 0 .$
Lucas numbers $L _ { n } = 2 , 1 , 3 , 4 , \ldots .$ satisfy $L _ { n } = L _ { n - 1 } + L _ { n - 2 } = \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { n } + \lambda _ { 2 } ^ { n } ,$ with $\lambda _ { 1 } ,$ $1 2 =$
$\left( 1 \pm \sqrt { 5 } \right) / 2$ from the Fibonacci matrix $\left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] .$ Compare $L _ { 0 } = 2$ with $F _ { 0 } = 0 .$
Markov matrix $M$ . All $m _ { i j } \geq 0$ and each column sum is 1. Largest eigenvalue $\lambda = 1$ 1. If
$m _ { i j } > 0 ,$ the columns of $M ^ { k }$ approach the steady state eigenvector $M s = s > 0 .$
☒

Matrix multiplication $A B .$ The $i ,$ $j$ entry of $A B$ is (row $i$ of A).(column $j$ of $\left. B \right) =$
$\sum a _ { i k } b _ { k j } .$ By columns: Column $j$ of $A B = A$ times column $j$ of $B .$ By rows: row
$i$ of $A$ multiplies $B .$ Columns times rows: $A B = \mathrm { s u m }$ of (column $\left. k \right) \left( r o w \quad k \right) .$ All
these equivalent definitions come from the rule that $A B$ times $x$ equals A times $B x .$
Minimal polynomial of A. The lowest degree polynomial with $m \left( A \right) = z e r o \quad m a t r i x .$ This
is $p \left( A \right) = \mathrm { d e t } \left( A - \lambda I \right)$ if no eigenvalues are repeated; always $m \left( 1 \right)$ divides $p \left( \lambda \right) .$
Multiplication $A x = x _ { 1 } \left( \text { column } 1 \right) + \cdots + x _ { n } \left( \text { column } n \right) = \text { com }$ of columns.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="561" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Glossary" -->

Multiplicities $A M$ and $G M .$ The algebraic multiplicity $A M$ of $2$ is the number of times
$\lambda$ appears as a root of $\mathrm { d e t } \left( A - \lambda I \right) = 0 .$ The geometric multiplicity GM is the
number of independent eigenvectors for $\lambda$ (= dimension of the eigenspace).

Multiplier $\ell _ { i j } .$ The pivot row $j$ is multiplied by $\ell _ { i j }$ and subtracted from row $i$ to eliminate
the $i ,$ $j$ entry: $\ell _ { i j } =$ (entry to eliminate) $/ \left( j \right.$ th pivot).

Network. A directed graph that has constants $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { m }$ associated with the edges.

Nilpotent matrix $N .$ Some power of $N$ is the zero matrix, $N ^ { k } = 0 .$ The only eigenvalue
is $\lambda = 0$ (repeated $n$ times). Examples: triangular matrices with zero diagonal.

Norm $\| A \| .$ The ${ } ^ { 6 6 } l ^ { 2 }$ norm" of $A$ is the maximum ratio $\| A x \| / \| x \| = \sigma _ { \max }$ . Then $\| A x \| \leq$
$\| A \| \| x \|$ and $\| A B \| \leq \| A \| \| B \|$ and $\| A + B \| \leq \| A \| + \| B \| .$ Frobenius norm
$\| A \| _ { F } ^ { 2 } = \sum \sum a _ { i j } ^ { 2 } .$ The $\ell ^ { 1 }$ and $l ^ { \infty }$ norms are largest column and row sums of $| a _ { i j } | .$
Normal equation $A ^ { T } A \widehat { x } = A ^ { T } b .$ Gives the least squares solution to $A x = b$ if A has full
rank $n$ (independent columns). The equation says that (columns of $A$ $\left. A \right) . \left( b - \quad A x \right) = 0 .$
Normal matrix. If $N N ^ { T } = N ^ { T } N ,$ then $N$ has orthonormal (complex) eigenvectors.

Nullspace $N \left( A \right) = A l l$ solutions to $A x = 0 .$ Dimension $n \quad - \quad r = \left( \# c o l u m n s \right) - \quad r a n k .$
Nullspace matrix $N .$ The columns of $N$ are the $n \quad - \quad r$ special solutions to $A s = 0 .$

Orthogonal matrix $Q .$ Square matrix with orthonormal columns, so $Q ^ { T } = Q ^ { - 1 } .$
Preserves length and angles, $\| Q x \| = \| x \|$ and $\left( Q x \right) ^ { T } \left( Q y \right) = x ^ { T } y .$ All $| \lambda | = 1 ,$
with orthogonal eigenvectors. Examples: Rotation, reflection, permutation.

Orthogonal subspaces. Every $v$ in $V$ is orthogonal to every $w$ in $W$ .

Orthonormal vectors $q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { n }$ . Dot products are $q _ { i } ^ { T } q _ { j } = 0$ if $i \neq j$ and $q _ { i } ^ { T } q _ { i } = 1 .$
The matrix $Q$ with these orthonormal columns has $Q ^ { T } Q = I$ . If $m = n$ then $Q ^ { T } =$
$Q ^ { - 1 }$ and $q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { n }$ is an orthonormal basis for $R ^ { n } :$ every $v = \sum \left( v ^ { T } q _ { j } \right) q _ { j } .$
Outer product $u v ^ { T } = c o l u m n$ times $r o w = r a n k$ one matrix.

Partial pivoting. In each column, choose the largest available pivot to control roundoff;
all multipliers have $| l _ { i j } | \leq 1 .$ See condition number.

Particular solution $x _ { p } .$ Any solution to $A x = b ;$ often $x _ { p }$ has free variables $= 0 .$

Pascal matrix $P _ { S } = \mathrm { p a s c a l } \left( n \right) =$ symmetric matrix with binomial entries $\left( \begin{array}{} { i + j - 2 } \\ { i - 1 } \end{array} \right) .$
$P _ { S } = P _ { L } P _ { U }$ all contain Pascal's triangle with $\mathrm { d e t } = 1$ (see Pascal in the index).

Permutation matrix $P .$ There are $n !$ orders of $1 , \ldots , n .$ The $n !$ P's have the rows of $I$ in
those orders. $P A$ puts the rows of A in the same order. $P$ is even or odd (det $P = 1$
$\left. \mathrm { o r } - 1 \right)$ based on the number of row exchanges to reach $I$ .

Pivot columns of A. Columns that contain pivots after row reduction. These are not
combinations of earlier columns. The pivot columns are a basis for the column space.
Pivot. The diagonal entry (first nonzero) at the time when a row is used in elimination.
Plane (or hyperplane) in $R ^ { n } .$ Vectors $x$ with $a ^ { T } x = 0 .$ Plane is perpendicular to $a \neq 0 .$
Polar decomposition $A = Q H .$ Orthogonal $Q$ times positive (semi)definite $H .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="562" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Glossary" -->

Positive definite matrix A. Symmetric matrix with positive eigenvalues and positive
pivots. Definition: $x ^ { T } A x > 0$ unless $x = 0 .$ Then $A = L D L ^ { T }$ with $\mathrm { d i a g } \left( D \right) > 0 .$
Projection $p = a \left( a ^ { T } b / a ^ { T } a \right)$ onto the line through $a .$ $P = a a ^ { T } / a ^ { T } a$ has rank 1.

Projection matrix $P$ onto subspace $\mathcal{S} .$ Projection $p = P b$ is the closest point to $b$ in
$\mathcal{S} ,$ error $e = b \quad - \quad P b$ is perpendicular to $S .$ $P ^ { 2 } = P = P ^ { T } ,$ eigenvalues are 1 or 0,
eigenvectors are in $S$ or $\bar { S } ^ { \perp } .$ If columns of $A = b a s i s$ for $\mathcal{S}$ then $P = A \left( A ^ { T } A \right) ^ { - 1 } A ^ { T } .$
Pseudoinverse $A ^ { + }$ (Moore-Penrose inverse). The $n$ by $m$ matrix that "inverts" A from
column space back to row space, with $N \left( A ^ { + } \right) = N \left( A ^ { T } \right) .$ $A ^ { + } A$ and $A A ^ { + }$ are the
projection matrices onto the row space and column space. $\mathrm { R a n k } \left( A ^ { + } \right) = \mathrm { r a n k } \left( A \right) .$

Random matrix $\mathrm { r a n d } \left( n \right)$ or randn(n). MATLAB creates a matrix with random entries,
uniformly distributed on $\left[ \begin{array}{} { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]$ for rand and standard normal distribution for randn.
Rank one matrix $A = u v ^ { T } \neq 0 .$ Column and row spaces = lines $c u$ and $c v .$

Rank $r \left( A \right) = n u m b e r$ of pivots = dimension of column space = dimension of row space.
Rayleigh quotient $q \left( x \right) = x ^ { T } A x / x ^ { T } x$ for symmetric $A : \lambda _ { \min } \leq q \left( x \right) \leq \lambda _ { \max } .$ Those
extremes are reached at the eigenvectors $x$ for $\lambda _ { \min } \left( A \right)$ and $\mathrm { l m a x } \left( A \right) .$

Reduced row echelon form $R = \mathrm { r r e f } \left( A \right) .$ Pivots $= 1 ;$ zeros above and below pivots; the
$r$ nonzero rows of $R$ give a basis for the row space of $A .$

Reflection matrix (Householder) $Q = I - 2 u u ^ { T } .$ Unit vector $u$ is reflected to $Q u = - \quad u .$
All $x$ in the plane mirror $u ^ { T } x = 0$ have $Q x = x .$ Notice $Q ^ { T } = Q ^ { - 1 } = Q .$

Right inverse $A ^ { + } .$ If $A$ has full row rank $m ,$ then $A ^ { + } = A ^ { T } \left( A A ^ { T } \right) ^ { - 1 }$ has $A A ^ { + } = I _ { m } .$
Rotation matrix $R = \left[ \begin{array}{} { c } & { - s } \\ { s } & { c } \end{array} \right]$ rotates the plane by $\theta$ and $R ^ { - 1 } = R ^ { T }$ rotates back by $- \theta .$
Eigenvalues are $e ^ { i \theta }$ and $e ^ { - i \theta } ,$ eigenvectors are $\left( 1 , \pm i \right) .$ c, $, s = \cos \theta$ , $\sin \theta .$

Row picture of $A x = b .$ Each equation gives a plane in $R ^ { n }$ the planes intersect at x.
Row space $C \left( A ^ { T } \right) = a l l$ combinations of rows of $A .$ Column vectors by convention.
Saddle point of $f \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } \right) .$ A point where the first derivatives of $f$ are zero and the
second derivative matrix $\left( \partial ^ { 2 } f / \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { j } = H e s \text { sian } m a t r i \right.$ is indefinite.
Schur complement $S = D - C A ^ { - 1 } B .$ Appears in block elimination on $\left[ \begin{array}{} \mathrm { A } & \mathrm { B } \\ \mathrm { C } & \mathrm { D } \end{array} \right] .$
Schwarz inequality | $| v \cdot w | \leq \| v \| \| w \| .$ Then $| v ^ { T } A w | ^ { 2 } \leq \left( v ^ { T } A v \right) \left( w ^ { T } A w \right)$ for pos def $A .$
Semidefinite matrix $A .$ (Positive) semidefinite: all $x ^ { T } A x \geq 0 ,$ all $\lambda \geq 0$ $A = \mathrm { a n y } R ^ { T } R .$
Similar matrices $A$ and $B .$ Every $B = M ^ { - 1 } A M$ has the same eigenvalues as A.

Simplex method for linear programming. The minimum cost vector $x ^ { * }$ is found by
moving from corner to lower cost corner along the edges of the feasible set (where
the constraints $A x = b$ and $x \geq 0$ are satisfied). Minimum cost at a corner!

Singular matrix A. A square matrix that has no inverse: $\mathrm { d e t } \left( A \right) = 0 .$

Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) A = UEVT = (orthogonal)(diag)(orthogonal)
First $r$ columns of $U$ and $V$ are orthonormal bases of $C \left( A \right)$ and $C \left( A ^ { T } \right) ,$ $A v _ { i } = \sigma _ { i } u _ { i }$
with singular value $\sigma _ { i } > 0 .$ Last columns are orthonormal bases of nullspaces.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="563" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Glossary" -->

Skew-symmetric matrix $K .$ The transpose is $- \quad K ,$ since $K _ { i j } = - K _ { j i } .$ Eigenvalues are
pure imaginary, eigenvectors are orthogonal, $e ^ { K t }$ is an orthogonal matrix.
Solvable system $A x = b .$ The right side $b$ is in the column space of A.

Spanning set. Combinations of $v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { m }$ fill the space. The columns of A span $C \left( A \right) !$
Special solutions to $A s = 0 .$ One free variable is $s _ { i } = 1 ,$ other free $v a r i a b l e s = 0 .$
Spectral Theorem $A = Q A Q ^ { T } .$ Real symmetric A has real A's and orthonormal q's.
Spectrum of $A = t h e \quad s e t$ of eigenvalues $\left\{ \lambda _ { 1 } , \ldots , \lambda _ { n } \right\} .$ Spectral $\mathrm { r a d i u s } = \max \mathrm { o f } | \lambda _ { i } | .$
Standard basis for $R ^ { n } .$ Columns of $n$ by $n$ identity matrix $\left( \mathrm { w r i t t e n } i , j , k \mathrm { i n } \mathrm { R } ^ { 3 } \right) .$
Stiffness matrix If $x$ gives the movements of the nodes, $K x$ gives the internal forces.

$K = A ^ { T } C A$ where $C$ has spring constants from Hooke's Law and $A x = s t r e t c h i n g .$
Subspace $S$ of $V$ . Any vector space inside $V ,$ including $V$ and $Z = z e r o \quad v e c t o r \quad o n l y .$
Sum $V + W$ of subspaces. Space of all $\left( v \quad i n \quad V \right) + \left( w \quad i n \quad W \right) .$ Direct sum: $V \cap W = \left\{ 0 \right\} .$
Symmetric factorizations $A = L D L ^ { T }$ and $A = Q A Q ^ { T } .$ Signs in $\Lambda = \text { signs in } D .$
Symmetric matrix $A .$ The transpose is $A ^ { T } = A ,$ and $a _ { i j } = a _ { j i } .$ $A ^ { - 1 }$ is also symmetric.
Toeplitz matrix. Constant down each $\mathrm { d i a g o n a l } = \mathrm { t i m e }$ time-invariant (shift-invariant) filter.
Trace of $A = \mathrm { s u m }$ of diagonal entries $= s u m$ of eigenvalues of A. Tr $A B = T r \quad B A .$
Transpose matrix $A ^ { T } .$ Entries $A _ { i j } ^ { T } = A _ { j i } .$ $A ^ { T }$ is $n$ by $m ,$ $A ^ { T } A$ is square, symmetric,
positive semidefinite. The transposes of $A B$ and $A ^ { - 1 }$ are $B ^ { T } A ^ { T }$ and $\left( A ^ { T } \right) ^ { - 1 } .$
Triangle inequality $\| u + v \| \leq \| u \| + \| v \| .$ For matrix norms $\| A + B \| \leq \| A \| + \| B \| .$
Tridiagonal matrix $T : t _ { i j } = 0$ if $| i \quad - \quad j | > 1 .$ $T ^ { - 1 }$ has rank 1 above and below diagonal.
Unitary matrix $U ^ { H } = \bar { U } ^ { T } = U ^ { - 1 } .$ Orthonormal columns (complex analog of $\left. Q \right) .$
Vandermonde matrix $V .$ $V c = b$ gives coefficients of $p \left( x \right) = c _ { 0 } + \cdots + c _ { n - 1 } x ^ { n - 1 }$
with $p \left( x _ { i } \right) = b _ { i } .$ $V _ { i j } = \left( x _ { i } \right) ^ { j - 1 }$ and det $V = \mathrm { p r o d u c t }$ of $\left( x _ { k } - x _ { i } \right)$ for $k >$ i.
Vector $v$ in $R ^ { n } .$ Sequence of $n$ real numbers $v = \left( v _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { n } \right) = \text { point in } R ^ { n } .$
Vector addition. $v + w = \left( v _ { 1 } + w _ { 1 } , \ldots , v _ { n } + w _ { n } \right) = \text { diagonal of } 1$ parallelogram.
Vector space $V .$ Set of vectors such that all combinations $c v + d w$ remain within $V$ .
Eight required rules are given in Section 3.1 for scalars $\mathcal{C} ,$ $d$ and vectors $v ,$ $w .$
Volume of box. The rows (or the columns) of $A$ generate $a$ box with volume $| \det \left( A \right) | .$
Wavelets $w _ { j k } \left( t \right) .$ Stretch and shift the time axis to create $w _ { j k } \left( t \right) = w _ { 0 0 } \left( 2 ^ { j } t - k \right) .$

<!-- PageBreak -->


## MATRIX FACTORIZATIONS

1\.

$A = L U = \left( \begin{array}{} \text { lower triangular } L \\ 1 ^ { \prime } \text { s on the diagonal } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \text { upper triangular } U \\ \text { pivots on the diagonal } \end{array} \right)$

Requirements: No row exchanges as Gaussian elimination reduces A to $U .$

2\.
$$A = L D U = \left( \begin{array}{} \text { lower triagular } L \\ \text { 1: } \text { on the diagonal } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \text { pivot matrix } \\ D \text { is diagonal } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \text { upper triangular } U \\ 1 ^ { \prime } \text { s on the diagonal } \end{array} \right)$$

Requirements: No row exchanges. The pivots in $D$ are divided out to leave 1's on
the diagonal of $U .$ If A is symmetric then $U$ is $L ^ { T }$ and $A = L D L ^ { T } .$

3\. $P A = L U$ (permutation matrix $P$ to avoid zeros in the pivot positions).

Requirements: $A$ is invertible. Then $P ,$ $L ,$ $U$ are invertible. $P$ does all of the
row exchanges in advance, to allow normal $L U .$ Alternative: $A = L _ { 1 } P _ { 1 } U _ { 1 } .$

4\. $E A = R$ (m by $m$ invertible $\left. E \right)$ (any matrix $\left. A \right) = \mathrm { r r e f } \left( A \right) .$

Requirements: None ! The reduced row echelon form $R$ has $r$ pivot rows and pivot
columns. The only nonzero in a pivot column is the unit pivot. The last $m \quad - \quad r$ rows
of $E$ are a basis for the left nullspace of A; they multiply A to give zero rows in $R .$
The first $r$ columns of $E ^ { - 1 }$ are $a$ basis for the column space of A.

5\. $A = C ^ { T } C = \left( \text { lower triangular } \right)$ (upper triangular) with $\sqrt { D }$ on both diagonals
Requirements: $A$ is symmetric and positive definite (all $n$ pivots in $D$ are positive).
This Cholesky factorization $C = \mathrm { c h o l } \left( A \right)$ has $C ^ { T } = L \sqrt { D } ,$ so $C ^ { T } C = L D L ^ { T } .$

6\. A = QR = (orthonormal columns in $\left. Q \right)$ (upper triangular $\left. R \right) .$

Requirements: A has independent columns. Those are orthogonalized in $Q$ by the
Gram-Schmidt or Householder process. If $A$ is square then $Q ^ { - 1 } = Q ^ { T } .$

7\. $A = S A S ^ { - 1 } = \left( \text { eigenvectors in } S \right)$ (eigenvalues in $\Lambda$ (left eigenvectors in $S ^ { - 1 } .$
Requirements: $A$ must have $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors.

8\. $A = Q A Q ^ { T } = \left( \text { orthogonal matrix } Q \right)$ (real eigenvalue matrix A) $Q ^ { T }$ is $Q ^ { - 1 } .$
Requirements: $A$ is real and symmetric. This is the Spectral Theorem.

<!-- PageNumber="564" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="565" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Matrix Factorizations" -->


### 9. $A = M J M ^ { - 1 } =$ eigenvectors in $\left. M \right)$ (Jordan blocks in $\left. J \right) \left( M ^ { - 1 } \right) .$

Requirements: A is any square matrix. This Jordan form $J$ has a block for each
independent eigenvector of A. Every block has only one eigenvalue.

10\.
$$A = U \Sigma V ^ { \mathrm { T } } = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { o r t h o g o n a l } \\ U \mathrm { i s } m \times n \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} m \times n \mathrm { s i n g u l a r } \mathrm { v a l u e } \mathrm { m a t r i x } \\ \sigma _ { 1 } , \ldots , \sigma _ { r } \mathrm { o n } \mathrm { i t s } \mathrm { d i a g o n a l } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { o r t h o g o n a l } \\ V \mathrm { i s } n \times n \end{array} \right)$$

Requirements: None. This singular value
tors of $A A ^ { T }$ in $U$ and eigenvectors of $A ^ { T } A$ in $V ;$ $\begin{array}{} { \text { mposition \left(SVD\right) has the eigenv } } \\ { \sigma _ { i } = \sqrt { \lambda _ { i } \left( A ^ { T } A \right) } = \sqrt { \lambda _ { i } \left( A A ^ { T } \right) } } \end{array} .$
☒ ☒

$$A ^ { + } = V \Sigma ^ { + } U ^ { \mathrm { T } } = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { o r t h o g o n a l } \\ n \times n \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} n \times m \mathrm { p s e u d o i n v e r s e } \mathrm { o f } \Sigma \\ 1 / \sigma _ { 1 } , \ldots , 1 / \sigma _ { r } \mathrm { o n } \mathrm { d i a g o n a l } \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { o r t h o g o n a l } \\ m \times m \end{array} \right)$$
11.

Requirements: None. The pseudoinverse $A ^ { + }$ has A+ A = projection onto row space
of $A$ and $A A ^ { + } =$ onto column space. The shortest least-squares solution
to $A x =$ bis $\widehat { x } = A ^ { + } b .$ This solves $A ^ { T } A \widehat { x } = A ^ { T } b .$


#### 12. $A = Q H = \left( o r t h o g o n a l \quad m a t r i x \right.$ $\left. Q \right)$ (symmetric positive definite matrix $\left. H \right) .$

Requirements: A is invertible. This polar decomposition has $H ^ { 2 } = A ^ { T } A .$ The
factor $H$ is semidefinite if A is singular. The reverse polar decomposition $A = K Q$
has $K ^ { 2 } = A A ^ { T } .$ Both have $Q = U V ^ { T }$ from the $S V D .$

13\. $A = U A U ^ { - 1 } = \left( \text { unitary } U \right)$ (eigenvalue matrix $\Lambda$ $\left( U ^ { - 1 } \right.$ which is $U ^ { H } = \bar { U } ^ { T } .$

Requirements: $A$ is normal: $A ^ { H } A = A A ^ { H } .$ Its orthonormal (and possibly complex)
eigenvectors are the columns of $U .$ Complex $\lambda$ unless $A = A ^ { H } :$ Hermitian case.

14\. $A = U T U ^ { - 1 } = \left( \text { unitary } U \right)$ (triangular $T$ with A's on diagonal) $\left( U ^ { - 1 } = U ^ { H } \right) .$

Requirements: Schur triangularization of any square $A .$ There is a matrix $U$ with
orthonormal columns that makes $U ^ { - 1 } A U$ triangular: Section 6.4.

15\. $F _ { n } = \left[ \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { D } \\ { I } & { - D } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{} { F _ { n / 2 } } & { \left. \\ & F _ { n / 2 } \end{array} \right] }$
even-odd
permutation
1
= one step of the (recursive) FFT.

Requirements: $F _ { n } = F$ Fourier matrix with entries $w ^ { j k }$ where $w ^ { n } = 1 : F _ { n } F _ { n } = n I .$
$D$ has $1 , w , \ldots , w ^ { n / 2 - 1 }$ on its diagonal. For $n = 2 ^ { \ell }$ the Fast Fourier Transform
will compute $F _ { n } x$ with only $\frac { 1 } { 2 } n \ell = \frac { 1 } { 2 } n \log _ { 2 } n$ multiplications from $\ell$ stages of D's.

## MATLAB TEACHING CODES

These Teaching Codes are directly available from web.mit.edu/ 18.06


<table>
<tr>
<td>cofactor</td>
<td>Compute the $n$ by $n$ matrix of cofactors.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>cramer</td>
<td>Solve the system $A x = b$ by Cramer's Rule.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>deter</td>
<td>Matrix determinant computed from the pivots in $P A = L U .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>eigen2</td>
<td>Eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and $\mathrm { d e t } \left( A - \lambda I \right)$ for 2 by 2 matrices.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>eigshow</td>
<td>Graphical demonstration of eigenvalues and singular values.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>eigval</td>
<td>Eigenvalues and their multiplicity as roots of $\mathrm { d e t } \left( A - \lambda I \right) = 0 .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>eigvec</td>
<td>Compute as many linearly independent eigenvectors as possible.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>elim</td>
<td>Reduction of $A$ to row echelon form $R$ by an invertible $E .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>findpiv</td>
<td>Find a pivot for Gaussian elimination (used by plu).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>fourbase</td>
<td>Construct bases for all four fundamental subspaces.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>grams</td>
<td>Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization of the columns of $A .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>house</td>
<td>2 by 12 matrix giving corner coordinates of a house.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>inverse</td>
<td>Matrix inverse (if it exists) by Gauss-Jordan elimination.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>leftnull</td>
<td>Compute a basis for the left nullspace.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>linefit</td>
<td>Plot the least squares fit to $m$ given points by a line.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Isq</td>
<td>Least squares solution to $A x = b$ from $A ^ { T } A \widehat { x } = A ^ { T } b .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>normal</td>
<td>Eigenvalues and orthonormal eigenvectors when $A ^ { T } A = A A ^ { T } .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>nulbasis</td>
<td>Matrix of special solutions to $A x = 0$ (basis for nullspace).</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>orthcomp</td>
<td>Find a basis for the orthogonal complement of a subspace.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>partic</td>
<td>Particular solution of $A x = b ,$ with all free variables zero.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$p l o t 2 d$</td>
<td>Two-dimensional plot for the house figures.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>plu</td>
<td>Rectangular $P A = L U$ factorization with row exchanges.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>poly2str</td>
<td>Express a polynomial as a string.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>project</td>
<td>Project a vector $b$ onto the column space of $A .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>projmat</td>
<td>Construct the projection matrix onto the column space of $A .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>randperm</td>
<td>Construct a random permutation.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>rowbasis</td>
<td>Compute a basis for the row space from the pivot rows of $R .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>samespan</td>
<td>Test whether two matrices have the same column space.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>signperm</td>
<td>Determinant of the permutation matrix with rows ordered by $p .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>słu</td>
<td>$L U$ factorization of a square matrix using no row exchanges.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>slv</td>
<td>Apply slu to solve the system $A x = b$ allowing no row exchanges.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>splu</td>
<td>Square $P A = L U$ factorization with row exchanges.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>splv</td>
<td>The solution to a square, invertible system $A x = b .$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>symmeig</td>
<td>Compute the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a symmetric matrix.</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>tridiag</td>
<td>Construct a tridiagonal matrix with constant diagonals $a ,$ $b ,$ c.</td>
</tr>
</table>

## LINEAR ALGEBRA IN A NUTSHELL ((The matrix A is $n$ by $n$ ))


<table>
<tr>
<th>Nonsingular</th>
<th>Singular</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$A$ is invertible</td>
<td>$A$ is not invertible</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The columns are independent</td>
<td>The columns are dependent</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The rows are independent</td>
<td>The rows are dependent</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The determinant is not zero</td>
<td>The determinant is zero</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$A x = 0$ has one solution $. x = 0$</td>
<td>$A x = 0$ has infinitely many solutions</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$A x = b$ has one solution $x = A ^ { - 1 } b$</td>
<td>$A x = b$ has no solution or infinitely many</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$A \quad h a s \quad n$ (nonzero) pivots</td>
<td>$A$ has $r < n$ pivots</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>A has full rank $r = n$</td>
<td>A has rank $r < n$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The reduced row echelon form is $R = I$</td>
<td>$R$ has at least one zero row</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The column space is all of $R ^ { n }$</td>
<td>The column space has dimension $r < n$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>The row space is all of $\mathbb{R} ^ { n }$</td>
<td>The row space has dimension $r < n$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>All eigenvalues are nonzero</td>
<td>Zero is an eigenvalue of $A$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$A ^ { T } A$ is symmetric positive definite</td>
<td>$A ^ { T } A$ is only semidefinite</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>A has $n$ (positive) singular values</td>
<td>$A$ has $r < n$ singular values</td>
</tr>
</table>


<!-- PageNumber="574" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

This book is designed to help students understan
central problems of linear algebra:

T0046757


<table>
<tr>
<td>$A x = b$</td>
<td>$n$ by n</td>
<td>Chapters 1-2</td>
<td>Linear systems</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$A x = b$</td>
<td>$m$ by $n$</td>
<td>Chapters 3-4</td>
<td>Least squares</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$A x \quad - \quad X x$</td>
<td>$n$ by n</td>
<td>Chapters 5-6</td>
<td>Eigenvalues</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>$A v = \sigma u$</td>
<td>$m$ by n</td>
<td>Chapters 6-7</td>
<td>Singular values</td>
</tr>
</table>


The diagram on the front cover shows the four fundamental subspaces
for the matrix $A .$ Those subspaces lead to the Fundamental Theorem
of Linear Algebra:

1\. The dimensions of the four subspaces

2\. The orthogonality of the two pairs

3\. The best bases for all four subspaces

This is the textbook that accompanies the author's video lectures
and the review material on MIT's OpenCourseWare.

ocw.mit.edu and web.mit.edu/18.06

Many universities and colleges (and now high schools) use this
textbook. Chapters 7-10 are for a second course on linear algebra.
